evict.def: expel (someone) from a property, especially with legal support.
evict.sentence: The landlord threatened to evict the tenants for nonpayment of rent.

evince.def: reveal the presence of (a quality or feeling); indicate.
evince.sentence: His calm demeanor evinced a deep inner strength.

evinced.def: past tense of evince; revealed or indicated clearly.
evinced.sentence: The student's essay evinced a thorough understanding of the topic.

evocative.def: Bringing strong images, memories, or feelings to mind.
evocative.sentence: The evocative scent of pine trees reminded her of childhood winters.

evoke.def: To bring or recall to the conscious mind.
evoke.sentence: The music evoked memories of a bygone era.

exacerbate.def: To make a problem, bad situation, or negative feeling worse.
exacerbate.sentence: The drought exacerbated the region's water shortage.

exacerbated.sentence: His exacerbated anxiety led to sleepless nights.

exact.def: To demand and obtain something, especially payment, from someone.
exact.sentence: The tyrant exacted heavy taxes from the peasants.

exacting.def: Making great demands on one's skill, attention, or other resources.
exacting.sentence: The exacting standards of the chef ensured every dish was perfect.

exaggerate.def: To represent something as being larger, greater, better, or worse than it really is.
exaggerate.sentence: He tends to exaggerate the size of the fish he caught.

exalt.def: To praise someone or something highly.
exalt.sentence: Critics exalted the novel as a masterpiece.

exasperate.def: To irritate or frustrate someone intensely.
exasperate.sentence: The constant delays exasperated the passengers.

exasperated.sentence: She let out an exasperated sigh when the computer crashed again.

excise.def: To remove something by cutting it out.
excise.sentence: The surgeon excised the tumor with precision.

excoriate.def: To criticize someone or something severely.
excoriate.sentence: The editorial excoriated the government's handling of the crisis.

excoriated.sentence: The excoriated policy was eventually withdrawn.

excruciating.sentence: The patient endured excruciating pain after the surgery.

exculpate.def: To show or declare that someone is not guilty of wrongdoing.
exculpate.sentence: The new evidence exculpated the defendant.

execrable.sentence: The meal was execrable, with overcooked vegetables and bland sauce.

exegesis.def: A critical explanation or interpretation of a text, especially a religious one.
exegesis.sentence: The scholar's exegesis of the biblical passage shed new light on its meaning.

exemplar.def: A person or thing serving as a typical example or excellent model.
exemplar.sentence: She was considered an exemplar of virtue in the community.

exemplary.def: Serving as a desirable model; very good.
exemplary.sentence: His exemplary behavior earned him a promotion.

exemplify.def: To be a typical example of something.
exemplify.sentence: This painting exemplifies the artist's early style.

exhaustive.sentence: The report provided an exhaustive analysis of the market trends.

exhilarating.def: Making one feel very happy, animated, or elated.
exhilarating.sentence: The exhilarating roller coaster ride left them breathless.

exhort.def: To strongly encourage or urge someone to do something.
exhort.sentence: The coach exhorted the team to give their best effort.

exigency.sentence: The exigency of the situation required immediate action.

exigent.def: Pressing or demanding; requiring immediate action.
exigent.sentence: The exigent circumstances forced the evacuation.

existential.def: Relating to existence or the nature of being.
existential.sentence: The philosopher's existential questions about life and death troubled his students.

exodus.sentence: The exodus of residents from the city after the hurricane overwhelmed the highways.

exonerate.def: To free from blame or guilt.
exonerate.sentence: The new evidence exonerated the defendant after years in prison.

exorbitant.sentence: The hotel charged an exorbitant fee for a simple room.

exorcise.def: To drive out an evil spirit or negative influence.
exorcise.sentence: The priest performed a ritual to exorcise the demon from the house.

exotic.def: Intriguingly unusual or different; from a foreign country.
exotic.sentence: She brought back exotic spices from her travels in Asia.

expansive.def: Covering a wide area; comprehensive or extensive.
expansive.sentence: The professor gave an expansive lecture covering centuries of history.

expatiate.def: To speak or write at length or in detail.
expatiate.sentence: The author expatiated on the themes of his novel during the interview.

expatriate.def: A person living outside their native country.
expatriate.sentence: Many expatriates gather at the international club to share experiences.

expedient.def: Convenient and practical, though possibly improper or immoral.
expedient.sentence: The politician chose the expedient path of lying to win the election.

expedite.def: To speed up the progress of something.
expedite.sentence: The manager asked the team to expedite the project to meet the deadline.

expel.def: To force someone to leave a place or organization.
expel.sentence: The school board voted to expel the student for repeated misconduct.

expend.def: To use up or spend resources such as time or energy.
expend.sentence: She expended all her savings on the renovation of the old house.

expiate.def: To make amends or atone for wrongdoing.
expiate.sentence: He tried to expiate his guilt by volunteering at the shelter.

explicable.def: Able to be explained or understood.
explicable.sentence: The phenomenon was explicable through scientific principles.

explicate.sentence: The teacher explicated the complex poem line by line.

explicit.def: Stated clearly and in detail, leaving no room for confusion.
explicit.sentence: The instructions were explicit about the safety precautions.

exploit.def: To make full use of something, often unfairly or selfishly.
exploit.sentence: The company exploited its workers by paying low wages.

exploitative.def: Making use of a situation or person in an unfair or selfish way.
exploitative.sentence: The exploitative contract left the artist with little profit.

exponent.def: A person who advocates or promotes an idea or theory.
exponent.sentence: She is a leading exponent of renewable energy policies.

exposition.def: A comprehensive explanation or description; a public exhibition.
exposition.sentence: The exposition of the scientific theory was clear and thorough.

expository.def: Intended to explain or describe something.
expository.sentence: The essay was expository, outlining the causes of the war.

expound.def: To present and explain a theory or idea in detail.
expound.sentence: The professor expounded on the principles of quantum mechanics.

expunge.sentence: The court ordered the record to be expunged from the database.

extant.sentence: Few manuscripts from that period are still extant.

extemporaneous.sentence: She gave an extemporaneous speech that impressed the audience.

extempore.sentence: He spoke extempore at the meeting.

extend.def: To make longer or larger; to offer.
extend.sentence: They decided to extend the deadline by a week.

extensive.sentence: The extensive research covered many topics.

extent.def: The degree or scope of something.
extent.sentence: To what extent are you willing to help?

extenuating.def: Lessening the seriousness of something, especially an offense.
extenuating.sentence: The judge considered the extenuating circumstances.

extirpate.def: To root out; to destroy completely.
extirpate.sentence: The goal is to extirpate the disease from the region.

extol.sentence: The teacher extolled the student's achievements.

extortion.def: The practice of obtaining something through force or threats.

extraneous.def: Irrelevant or unrelated to the subject.

extrapolate.def: To estimate or infer based on known data.
extrapolate.sentence: We can extrapolate future trends from past data.

extrapolation.def: The act of estimating or inferring based on known data.
extrapolation.sentence: His extrapolation of the results was accurate.

extravagant.sentence: She bought an extravagant diamond necklace.

extrinsic.def: Not part of the essential nature; external.
extrinsic.sentence: The value of the coin is extrinsic, based on its rarity.

exuberant.def: Full of energy, excitement, and cheerfulness.
exuberant.sentence: The exuberant children played in the park.

exult.def: To show or feel triumphant joy.
exult.sentence: The team exulted after winning the championship.

exultation.def: A feeling of triumphant elation or jubilation.

fabricate.def: To invent or concoct something, often with deceit; to construct.
fabricate.sentence: He fabricated an excuse for being late.

facade.def: The front of a building; a deceptive outward appearance.
facade.sentence: Behind her cheerful facade, she was sad.

facet.def: One side or aspect of something.
facet.sentence: We examined every facet of the problem.

facetious.def: Treating serious issues with inappropriate humor.
facetious.sentence: His facetious remarks were not appreciated.

facile.sentence: The solution seemed too facile to be correct.

facilitate.sentence: The new software will facilitate data analysis.

faction.def: A small, dissenting group within a larger one.
faction.sentence: A faction within the party opposed the leader.

factious.def: Given to causing dissension or internal conflict.
factious.sentence: The factious committee members could never agree on a single proposal.

factitious.def: Artificially created or developed, not natural.
factitious.sentence: The factitious enthusiasm of the crowd was orchestrated by the marketing team.

factotum.def: A person employed to do all kinds of work.
factotum.sentence: He served as a factotum, handling everything from errands to bookkeeping.

faddish.sentence: Her faddish diet lasted only a few weeks before she moved on to the next trend.

fair-weather friends.def: Friends who are only supportive during good times.
fair-weather friends.sentence: When he lost his job, he discovered who his fair-weather friends were.

fallacious.def: Based on a mistaken belief or unsound reasoning.
fallacious.sentence: His fallacious argument convinced no one with a background in logic.

fallacy.def: A mistaken belief, especially one based on unsound argument.
fallacy.sentence: The idea that money brings happiness is a common fallacy.

fallible.def: Capable of making mistakes or being wrong.
fallible.sentence: Even the most experienced surgeon is fallible.

fallow.def: Left unplanted or unused to restore fertility or productivity.
fallow.sentence: Farmers often leave fields fallow to allow nutrients to replenish.

falter.def: To lose strength or momentum; to hesitate.
falter.sentence: His voice began to falter as he recalled the tragic event.

fanatic.def: A person with extreme and uncritical enthusiasm or zeal.
fanatic.sentence: The religious fanatic would not listen to any opposing views.

fanatical.def: Marked by excessive enthusiasm and often intense uncritical devotion.
fanatical.sentence: Her fanatical devotion to the cause alienated her friends.

fanciful.sentence: The architect's fanciful designs were beautiful but impractical.

fancy.def: To imagine or suppose; to have a desire for.
fancy.sentence: I fancy a cup of tea and a quiet afternoon.

farce.def: A ridiculous or absurd event or situation.
farce.sentence: The trial was a farce, with no evidence and a biased judge.

fastidious.def: Very attentive to detail and accuracy; hard to please.
fastidious.sentence: The fastidious editor caught every typo in the manuscript.

fat is in the fire.def: Trouble or disaster is imminent or has begun.
fat is in the fire.sentence: When the boss saw the missing files, he knew the fat was in the fire.

fatal.sentence: The driver's fatal mistake was texting while driving.

fathom.def: To understand or comprehend after much thought.
fathom.sentence: I cannot fathom why she would lie about something so trivial.

fatuous.sentence: His fatuous comments during the meeting wasted everyone's time.

fauna.def: The animals of a particular region, habitat, or geological period.
fauna.sentence: The island's fauna includes several species found nowhere else.

fawn.def: To show flattering affection or servile deference.
fawn.sentence: The assistant would fawn over the celebrity, hoping for a favor.

fawning.sentence: The fawning reviews of the movie were clearly paid for.

feasible.def: Possible and practical to do easily.
feasible.sentence: The plan is feasible if we have enough funding and time.

feckless.def: Lacking initiative or strength of character; irresponsible.
feckless.sentence: The feckless manager never followed through on any project.

fecund.def: Producing or capable of producing abundant offspring or vegetation; fertile.
fecund.sentence: The fecund soil of the valley yielded three harvests each year.

feeble.def: Lacking physical strength, especially as a result of age or illness; weak.
feeble.sentence: After the long illness, his voice was barely a feeble whisper.

feeling no pain.sentence: After three glasses of wine, he was feeling no pain and started telling jokes.

feign.def: Pretend to be affected by (a feeling, state, or injury).
feign.sentence: She feigned surprise when she saw the cake, though she had planned the party.

feint.def: A deceptive or pretended blow, thrust, or movement, especially in boxing or fencing.
feint.sentence: The boxer made a feint with his left hand before landing a right hook.

felicitate.def: Congratulate; express pleasure at someone's good fortune.
felicitate.sentence: I felicitated her on her promotion to vice president.

felicitous.def: Well chosen or suited to the circumstances; apt and pleasing.
felicitous.sentence: Her felicitous remark broke the tension in the room.

felicity.def: Intense happiness; the quality of being pleasing and appropriate.
felicity.sentence: The couple lived in felicity for many years.

fell.def: Of terrible evil or ferocity; deadly.
fell.sentence: The knight faced a fell beast in the dark forest.

felon.def: A person who has been convicted of a felony.
felon.sentence: The felon was sentenced to ten years in prison.

feral.def: Existing in a wild state, especially after escaping from domestication.
feral.sentence: The island is home to a population of feral cats.

ferment.def: A state of agitation or unrest; a process of chemical change caused by enzymes.
ferment.sentence: The political ferment in the country led to widespread protests.

ferret.def: Search out and discover information, often by persistent investigation.
ferret.sentence: The journalist ferreted out the truth behind the scandal.

fervent.def: Having or displaying a passionate intensity.
fervent.sentence: She was a fervent supporter of animal rights.

fervid.def: Intensely enthusiastic or passionate, especially to an excessive degree.
fervid.sentence: His fervid speeches often stirred the crowd to action.

fervor.sentence: The team played with great fervor and won the championship.

fester.def: Become infected and suppurate; (of a negative feeling) become worse or more intense.
fester.sentence: The wound began to fester without proper treatment.

festoon.def: Adorn (a place) with chains, garlands, or other decorations.
festoon.sentence: The hall was festooned with ribbons and balloons for the party.

fete.sentence: The village held a fete to raise money for the church.

fetid.sentence: The fetid odor from the swamp made us cover our noses.

fetish.def: A form of sexual desire in which gratification is linked to an object or body part; an excessive and irrational devotion.
fetish.sentence: He had a fetish for antique watches.

fetter.def: A chain or shackle placed around a prisoner's ankles; something that confines or restrains.
fetter.sentence: The prisoner was kept in fetters.

fetter (v).def: Restrain with chains or shackles, typically around the ankles; confine or restrict.
fetter (v).sentence: The new regulations fetter the company's ability to expand.

feud.def: A prolonged and bitter quarrel or dispute.
feud.sentence: The two families have been in a feud for generations.

fiasco.def: A thing that is a complete failure, especially in a ludicrous or humiliating way.
fiasco.sentence: The outdoor concert was a fiasco due to the sudden rainstorm.

fiat.sentence: The king issued a fiat that all citizens must pay a new tax.

fickle.def: Changing frequently, especially in loyalties or affections.
fickle.sentence: Her fickle taste in music made it hard to buy her a gift.

fidelity.def: Faithfulness to a person, cause, or belief, demonstrated by continuing loyalty and support.
fidelity.sentence: The dog's fidelity to its owner was unwavering.

fidget.def: To make small, restless movements, especially due to nervousness or impatience.
fidget.sentence: The child began to fidget in his seat during the long lecture.

figurative.def: Departing from a literal meaning; metaphorical.
figurative.sentence: The phrase 'break a leg' is a figurative expression meaning good luck.

filial.def: Relating to or due from a son or daughter.
filial.sentence: He felt a strong filial duty to care for his aging parents.

filibuster.def: A prolonged speech that obstructs progress in a legislative assembly.
filibuster.sentence: The senator's filibuster delayed the vote for hours.

filigree.def: Ornamental work of fine wire, often used in jewelry.
filigree.sentence: The antique brooch was made of delicate gold filigree.

fillip.def: A stimulus or boost; something that adds excitement or energy.
fillip.sentence: The new policy gave a fillip to the struggling economy.

finagle.def: To obtain something through trickery or clever manipulation.
finagle.sentence: He managed to finagle a free ticket to the concert.

finagled.def: Past tense of finagle; obtained through trickery or clever manipulation.
finagled.sentence: She finagled her way into the exclusive party.

finesse.def: Skillful handling of a situation; delicate and refined technique.
finesse.sentence: She handled the difficult negotiation with great finesse.

finicky.def: Excessively particular or fussy about details.
finicky.sentence: My cat is a finicky eater and only likes one brand of food.

firebrand.def: A person who stirs up trouble or revolution.
firebrand.sentence: The political firebrand rallied the crowd with his fiery speech.

fissure.def: A narrow opening or crack, especially in rock or earth.
fissure.sentence: Water seeped through a fissure in the rock.

fitful.def: Occurring irregularly; not steady or continuous.
fitful.sentence: The baby's sleep was fitful, waking every hour.

flabbergasted.sentence: I was flabbergasted by the unexpected news.

flag.def: To lose strength or vigor; to become weak or less energetic.
flag.sentence: As the race continued, his energy began to flag.

flagrant.sentence: The referee ignored a flagrant foul.

flamboyant.def: Tending to attract attention because of exuberance, confidence, or stylishness.
flamboyant.sentence: The flamboyant actor wore a bright purple suit to the premiere.

flatter.sentence: He tried to flatter his boss to get a promotion.

flaunt.def: To display ostentatiously or show off.
flaunt.sentence: She loved to flaunt her expensive jewelry.

flay.def: To strip off the skin of an animal; to criticize severely.
flay.sentence: The critics flayed the director's latest film.

fledging.def: Alternative spelling of fledgling; inexperienced or new.
fledging.sentence: The fledging journalist was eager to cover her first big story.

fledgling.sentence: The fledgling company struggled to compete with industry giants.

fleece.def: to deceive or swindle someone out of money.
fleece.sentence: The con artist managed to fleece the elderly couple out of their life savings.

fleeting.def: lasting for a very short time.
fleeting.sentence: The beauty of the sunset was fleeting, disappearing within minutes.

flimsy.def: lacking solidity or strength; easily broken or damaged.
flimsy.sentence: The flimsy cardboard box collapsed under the weight of the books.

flinch.def: to make a quick, nervous movement as an instinctive reaction to fear, pain, or surprise.
flinch.sentence: He didn't flinch when the doctor gave him the injection.

flip.def: to turn over or cause to turn over with a sudden quick movement.
flip.sentence: She flipped the pancake expertly in the pan.

flippant.def: not showing a serious or respectful attitude.
flippant.sentence: His flippant remarks during the meeting offended several colleagues.

flirt.def: to behave as if attracted to someone, often in a playful or insincere way.
flirt.sentence: She would often flirt with the barista to get a free coffee.

flop.def: a complete failure, especially in entertainment or business.
flop.sentence: The movie was a major flop, earning less than its production cost.

flora.def: the plants of a particular region, habitat, or geological period.
flora.sentence: The flora of the Amazon rainforest is incredibly diverse.

florid.def: having a red or flushed complexion; also, excessively elaborate or ornate.
florid.sentence: His florid face indicated he had been running.

flotilla.def: a fleet of boats or small ships.
flotilla.sentence: A flotilla of fishing boats set out at dawn.

flotsam.def: the wreckage of a ship or its cargo found floating on the sea.
flotsam.sentence: After the storm, the beach was littered with flotsam.

flounder.def: to struggle or be in trouble, especially in moving or making progress.
flounder.sentence: Without a clear plan, the project began to flounder.

flourish.def: to grow or develop in a healthy or vigorous way.
flourish.sentence: The plants flourished in the warm, sunny climate.

flout.def: to openly disregard a rule, law, or convention.
flout.sentence: He flouted the speed limit by driving 100 mph in a residential area.

flouted.def: past tense of flout; openly disregarded a rule or convention.
flouted.sentence: The company flouted safety regulations, leading to a fine.

fluctuate.def: to rise and fall irregularly in number or amount.
fluctuate.sentence: Stock prices fluctuate daily based on market conditions.

fluffy.def: soft and light, like fur or feathers.
fluffy.sentence: The kitten had fluffy white fur that was delightful to pet.

fluke.def: an unlikely chance occurrence, especially a lucky one.
fluke.sentence: Winning the lottery was a complete fluke.

flummox.sentence: The complex puzzle flummoxed even the experts.

flummoxed.sentence: She was flummoxed by the sudden change in plans.

flush.def: having plenty of money; also, red in the face.
flush.sentence: After winning the bet, he was flush with cash.

flustered.def: agitated or confused, especially due to being hurried or under pressure.
flustered.sentence: She became flustered when asked to speak impromptu.

flux.def: a state of continuous change or movement.
flux.sentence: The company's policies are in flux due to the merger.

foible.def: a minor weakness or eccentricity in someone's character.
foible.sentence: His only foible is an obsession with collecting vintage watches.

foil.def: to prevent from succeeding; to thwart
foil.sentence: The security team foiled the attempted robbery.

foist.def: to impose something unwanted on someone
foist.sentence: He tried to foist his old computer on his younger brother.

foment.def: to stir up or incite trouble or rebellion
foment.sentence: The agitator was arrested for fomenting unrest among the workers.

foolhardy.sentence: It was foolhardy to go hiking in the storm without proper gear.

foppish.def: excessively concerned with fashion and appearance
foppish.sentence: His foppish attire and affected mannerisms made him stand out at the rustic gathering.

forage.def: to search for food or provisions
forage.sentence: The deer foraged in the forest for berries and tender shoots.

forbear.def: to refrain from doing something; to be patient
forbear.sentence: She decided to forbear from criticizing his mistake.

forbearance.sentence: The teacher showed great forbearance with the disruptive student.

ford.def: a shallow place in a river that can be crossed
ford.sentence: They crossed the river at a ford where the water was only knee-deep.

foreboding.def: a feeling that something bad will happen
foreboding.sentence: A sense of foreboding settled over the town as the storm approached.

foreclosure.def: the legal process of taking possession of a property when a loan is not repaid
foreclosure.sentence: The bank initiated foreclosure proceedings after months of missed mortgage payments.

foreground.sentence: The documentary foregrounds the struggles of immigrant workers.

foreseeable.def: able to be predicted or anticipated
foreseeable.sentence: In the foreseeable future, electric cars will become more common.

foreshadow.def: to indicate or suggest something that will happen later
foreshadow.sentence: The dark clouds foreshadowed the coming storm.

forestall.def: to prevent or delay something by taking action in advance
forestall.sentence: The government raised interest rates to forestall inflation.

forfeit.def: to lose or give up something as a penalty
forfeit.sentence: He forfeited his deposit by canceling the reservation at the last minute.

forge.def: to shape metal by heating and hammering; to create with effort
forge.sentence: The blacksmith forged a beautiful iron gate.

forgery.def: the act of falsely making or altering a document or signature
forgery.sentence: He was arrested for forgery after trying to cash a fake check.

forlorn.sentence: The forlorn puppy sat alone in the rain.

formidable.def: inspiring fear or respect through being impressively powerful
formidable.sentence: The team faced a formidable opponent in the championship game.

forsake.def: to abandon or give up someone or something
forsake.sentence: He forsook his family to pursue a life of adventure.

forswear.def: to renounce or give up under oath
forswear.sentence: The witness forswore any knowledge of the crime.

forte.def: a person's strong point or talent
forte.sentence: Mathematics was never her forte; she excelled in literature.

forthcoming.def: about to happen or appear; available when needed
forthcoming.sentence: The author's forthcoming novel is highly anticipated.

forthright.sentence: She gave a forthright opinion about the company's policies.

forthwith.sentence: The manager demanded that the report be submitted forthwith.

fortify.def: To strengthen or reinforce, especially with additional defenses or nutrients.
fortify.sentence: The soldiers worked to fortify the castle walls before the siege.

fortitude.def: Courage and strength in the face of adversity or pain.
fortitude.sentence: She endured the long illness with remarkable fortitude.

fortuitous.def: Happening by accident or chance, often with a fortunate outcome.
fortuitous.sentence: Their meeting was fortuitous, leading to a successful partnership.

forty winks.def: A short nap, especially during the day.
forty winks.sentence: He took forty winks after lunch to refresh himself.

foster.def: To encourage or promote the development of something.
foster.sentence: The teacher aimed to foster a love of reading in her students.

founder.def: To fail or collapse, especially of a plan or enterprise.
founder.sentence: The business began to founder after the market crash.

fracas.def: A noisy, disorderly fight or quarrel.
fracas.sentence: A fracas broke out in the bar after closing time.

fractious.def: Irritable and quarrelsome; difficult to control.
fractious.sentence: The fractious child refused to obey his parents.

frail.def: Physically weak or delicate; easily broken.
frail.sentence: The frail old man needed assistance to walk.

frailty.def: The condition of being weak or delicate; a moral weakness.
frailty.sentence: Human frailty is a common theme in literature.

fraternize.def: To associate or socialize in a friendly way, often with someone considered an enemy.
fraternize.sentence: The soldiers were forbidden to fraternize with the locals.

fraud.def: Wrongful deception intended to result in financial or personal gain.
fraud.sentence: He was convicted of fraud for falsifying documents.

fraught.def: Filled with or accompanied by something undesirable; causing anxiety.
fraught.sentence: The negotiations were fraught with tension.

fray.sentence: He jumped into the fray to defend his friend.

frenetic.def: Fast and energetic in a wild, uncontrolled way.
frenetic.sentence: The frenetic pace of the city exhausted her.

frenzy.def: A state of wild excitement or agitation.
frenzy.sentence: The crowd worked themselves into a frenzy.

fresco.def: A painting done on fresh plaster, typically on a wall or ceiling.
fresco.sentence: The chapel ceiling was adorned with a beautiful fresco.

fret.def: To be constantly or visibly worried or anxious.
fret.sentence: She fretted over the upcoming exam.

fretful.def: Feeling or expressing distress or irritation.
fretful.sentence: The fretful baby cried all night.

frieze.def: A decorative horizontal band, especially on a building or wall.
frieze.sentence: The ancient temple had a frieze depicting battles.

fringe.def: An ornamental border of threads or tassels; the outer edge of something.
fringe.sentence: She wore a scarf with a long fringe.

frivolous.def: Not having any serious purpose or value; carefree and lighthearted.
frivolous.sentence: He spent his money on frivolous purchases.

from pillar to post.def: From one place to another in a restless or aimless manner.
from pillar to post.sentence: After losing his job, he went from pillar to post looking for work.

froward.def: Stubbornly contrary and disobedient; difficult to deal with.
froward.sentence: The froward child refused to follow any instructions.

frown.def: to wrinkle one's brow in displeasure or concentration
frown.sentence: She frowned at the messy report.

frugal.def: sparing or economical with regard to money or food
frugal.sentence: He lived a frugal life, saving every penny.

frugality.def: the quality of being thrifty and economical
frugality.sentence: Her frugality allowed her to retire early.

fruitful.sentence: The collaboration was fruitful and led to many innovations.

fruition.def: the realization or fulfillment of a plan or project
fruition.sentence: After years of work, his dream came to fruition.

fruitless.def: failing to achieve the desired results; unproductive
fruitless.sentence: Their fruitless search for treasure ended in disappointment.

frustrate.def: to cause feelings of discouragement or annoyance
frustrate.sentence: The constant delays frustrated the team.

fudge.def: to present or manipulate something in a vague or dishonest way
fudge.sentence: He fudged the numbers to make the report look better.

fulcrum.def: the point on which a lever rests or is supported
fulcrum.sentence: The fulcrum of the seesaw is at the center.

fulminate.def: to express vehement protest or criticism
fulminate.sentence: The senator fulminated against the new policy.

fulsome.sentence: His fulsome praise made her uncomfortable.

funereal.def: having the mournful, somber character of a funeral
funereal.sentence: The room had a funereal atmosphere after the bad news.

fungible.def: able to be replaced or exchanged for something else
fungible.sentence: Money is fungible; one dollar is as good as another.

furor.def: an outbreak of public anger or excitement
furor.sentence: The decision caused a furor among the citizens.

furtive.def: attempting to avoid notice or attention; secretive
furtive.sentence: He cast a furtive glance at the door.

fusion.def: the process of combining two or more things into a single entity
fusion.sentence: The fusion of jazz and rock created a new genre.

futile.def: incapable of producing any useful result; pointless
futile.sentence: All attempts to revive him were futile.

futility.def: the quality of being futile; uselessness
futility.sentence: He realized the futility of arguing with her.

gadfly.def: a person who provokes or annoys others, especially by persistent criticism
gadfly.sentence: The gadfly at the town meeting questioned every proposal.

gaffe.def: an unintentional social blunder or awkward remark
gaffe.sentence: His gaffe about her age was embarrassing.

gainsay.def: to deny or contradict a statement or fact
gainsay.sentence: No one could gainsay the evidence presented.

gall.sentence: She had the gall to ask for a raise after arriving late.

gallant.def: brave, heroic, or chivalrous in manner
gallant.sentence: The gallant knight rescued the princess.

galvanize.def: to shock or excite someone into taking action
galvanize.sentence: The speech galvanized the crowd into action.

gambit.def: a strategic move or opening, often involving a sacrifice
gambit.sentence: His opening gambit in the negotiation was to offer a discount.

gambol.sentence: The lambs gamboled in the meadow, full of energy.

gamut.def: the complete range or scope of something
gamut.sentence: Her emotions ran the gamut from joy to despair.

garble.def: to distort or confuse a message or sound
garble.sentence: The radio transmission was garbled by static.

garbled.def: confused or distorted, especially in sound or meaning
garbled.sentence: His garbled explanation only added to the confusion.

gargantuan.sentence: The gargantuan cruise ship dwarfed the small harbor.

garner.def: to gather or collect something, often information or support
garner.sentence: The politician garnered widespread support for her proposal.

garrulous.def: excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters
garrulous.sentence: The garrulous neighbor kept me on the phone for an hour.

gauche.def: lacking social grace; awkward or tactless
gauche.sentence: His gauche comments at the dinner party embarrassed everyone.

gaudy.sentence: She wore a gaudy sequined dress that clashed with everything.

gaunt.def: extremely thin and bony, as from hunger or illness
gaunt.sentence: After weeks without food, his face looked gaunt and hollow.

gauntlet.def: a glove with extended cuff, or a challenge or ordeal
gauntlet.sentence: He threw down the gauntlet, challenging his rival to a duel.

gavel.def: a small mallet used by a judge or auctioneer to signal attention
gavel.sentence: The judge banged the gavel to restore order in the courtroom.

gawky.sentence: The gawky teenager tripped over his own feet.

genial.sentence: The genial host made everyone feel welcome.

geniality.def: the quality of being friendly and cheerful
geniality.sentence: His geniality put the nervous guests at ease.

genocide.def: the deliberate killing of a large group of people, especially a particular ethnic or national group
genocide.sentence: The world vowed never to allow another genocide.

genre.def: a category of artistic composition characterized by similarities in form, style, or subject matter
genre.sentence: Science fiction is my favorite literary genre.

genteel.def: polite, refined, and often associated with high social class
genteel.sentence: She spoke with a genteel accent that hinted at her upbringing.

germane.def: relevant and appropriate to the subject
germane.sentence: Your comments are not germane to the discussion.

germinate.def: to begin to grow or develop
germinate.sentence: Seeds need warmth and moisture to germinate.

gerrymander.def: to manipulate the boundaries of an electoral district to favor one party
gerrymander.sentence: The party was accused of gerrymandering to secure more seats.

gestation.def: the process of carrying or developing something, especially an idea or fetus
gestation.sentence: The gestation period for elephants is nearly two years.

gesticulate.def: to use gestures, especially dramatic ones, instead of or in addition to speech
gesticulate.sentence: He gesticulated wildly to emphasize his point.

ghastly.def: causing great horror or fear; extremely unpleasant
ghastly.sentence: The ghastly scene left the witnesses in shock.

gild.def: to cover thinly with gold, or to give a pleasing but deceptive appearance
gild.sentence: The artist gilded the frame to make it look more valuable.

gild the lily.def: To add unnecessary ornamentation or embellishment to something already beautiful or perfect.
gild the lily.sentence: Adding more decorations to the elegant cake would be gilding the lily.

gird.def: To prepare oneself for action or a confrontation.
gird.sentence: The team girded themselves for the challenging negotiations ahead.

gist.def: The central idea or essence of a matter.
gist.sentence: I didn't catch every detail, but I got the gist of her argument.

glacial.def: Extremely cold or slow-moving, like a glacier.
glacial.sentence: His glacial stare made everyone in the room uncomfortable.

glancing.def: Striking at an angle rather than squarely; brief or indirect.
glancing.sentence: The glancing blow from the branch left a small scratch on his arm.

glaring.def: Staring fiercely or angrily; also, conspicuously obvious or offensive.
glaring.sentence: The glaring error in the report was impossible to ignore.

glean.def: To gather information or material bit by bit.
glean.sentence: From the scattered clues, the detective gleaned the truth about the crime.

glib.def: Fluent and voluble but insincere and shallow.
glib.sentence: The politician's glib answers failed to convince the skeptical audience.

gloat.def: To dwell on one's own success or another's misfortune with smugness or malicious pleasure.
gloat.sentence: After winning the match, he couldn't help but gloat over his opponent's defeat.

glower.def: To look or stare with sullen dislike, anger, or contempt.
glower.sentence: The teacher glowered at the students who were whispering during the exam.

glowered.def: Past tense of glower; looked with sullen anger.
glowered.sentence: He glowered at the messenger who brought the bad news.

glum.sentence: After hearing the disappointing results, she sat in glum silence.

glut.sentence: The market saw a glut of oil, causing prices to plummet.

gnostic.def: Relating to knowledge, especially esoteric mystical knowledge.
gnostic.sentence: The gnostic teachings emphasized direct spiritual insight over blind faith.

go against the grain.def: To act contrary to what is normal or expected.
go against the grain.sentence: Her decision to quit a stable job and travel the world went against the grain.

goad.def: To provoke or annoy someone so as to stimulate some action or reaction.
goad.sentence: The constant criticism goaded him into proving his worth.

goosebumps.def: A prickling sensation on the skin caused by cold, fear, or excitement.
goosebumps.sentence: The horror movie gave me goosebumps.

gorge.def: To eat greedily or to excess.
gorge.sentence: After fasting all day, he gorged himself on pizza and cake.

gossamer.def: A fine, filmy substance consisting of cobwebs; also, something light, delicate, or insubstantial.
gossamer.sentence: The bride's veil was made of gossamer, floating ethereally around her.

gouge.def: To make a hole or groove in something, often with a sharp tool; also, to overcharge or swindle.
gouge.sentence: The contractor gouged the homeowner by charging exorbitant fees for shoddy work.

gradation.def: A process or change taking place through a series of stages, by degrees, or gradually.
gradation.sentence: The painting showed a beautiful gradation of colors from blue to green.

graft.def: The acquisition of money or power through dishonest or unethical means; also, a piece of living tissue transplanted surgically.
graft.sentence: The politician was convicted of graft after accepting bribes from contractors.

grandeur.def: Splendor and impressiveness, especially of appearance or style.
grandeur.sentence: The grandeur of the palace left the tourists in awe.

grandiloquent.def: Pompous or extravagant in language, style, or manner, especially in a way that is intended to impress.
grandiloquent.sentence: His grandiloquent speech was full of fancy words but said very little.

grandiose.def: Impressive or magnificent in appearance or style, often to an exaggerated or unrealistic degree.
grandiose.sentence: The architect's grandiose plans for a skyscraper were deemed too expensive to build.

grandstand.def: to behave dramatically to attract attention or impress an audience.
grandstand.sentence: The politician grandstanded during the debate, making exaggerated gestures to win over the crowd.

grasping.def: greedy, especially for money or material possessions.
grasping.sentence: The grasping landlord raised the rent every year without making any improvements.

gratify.def: to give pleasure or satisfaction to someone.
gratify.sentence: It gratified her to see her students succeed.

grating.def: irritating or unpleasant, especially referring to a sound or manner.
grating.sentence: His grating voice made it hard to concentrate on the lecture.

gratis.sentence: The hotel offers a complimentary breakfast gratis to all guests.

gratitude.def: a feeling of thankfulness and appreciation.
gratitude.sentence: She expressed her gratitude with a heartfelt thank-you note.

gratuitous.sentence: The movie was criticized for its gratuitous violence.

gratuity.sentence: He left a generous gratuity for the waiter.

gravel.def: small stones used for paths and roads.
gravel.sentence: The driveway was covered with loose gravel.

gravitas.def: dignity, seriousness, or solemnity of manner.
gravitas.sentence: The judge spoke with gravitas as she delivered the verdict.

gravity.def: the force that attracts objects toward the center of the Earth, or extreme importance.
gravity.sentence: He didn't seem to understand the gravity of the situation.

grazing.def: the act of animals feeding on grass in a field.
grazing.sentence: Cows were grazing peacefully in the meadow.

gregarious.sentence: She was a gregarious person who loved hosting parties.

grievance.def: a real or imagined cause for complaint, especially unfair treatment.
grievance.sentence: The workers filed a grievance about unsafe working conditions.

grievous.def: causing great pain, suffering, or sorrow; very serious.
grievous.sentence: The accident caused grievous injuries to several passengers.

grill.def: to question someone intensely and persistently.
grill.sentence: The detective grilled the suspect for hours.

grimace.def: a facial expression of pain, disgust, or disapproval.
grimace.sentence: He made a grimace when he tasted the sour lemon.

grisly.sentence: The crime scene was grisly, with blood everywhere.

grotesque.def: comically or repulsively ugly or distorted.
grotesque.sentence: The Halloween decorations were intentionally grotesque.

grouse.def: to complain or grumble about something trivial.
grouse.sentence: He spent the whole meeting grousing about the office temperature.

grovel.def: to act obsequiously in order to obtain forgiveness or favor.
grovel.sentence: He groveled before his boss, begging for another chance.

grudging.def: given or allowed only reluctantly or resentfully.
grudging.sentence: She offered a grudging apology after being proven wrong.

grumble.def: to complain in a low, surly manner.
grumble.sentence: The customers grumbled about the long wait.

guarded.def: cautious and reserved in manner or speech.
guarded.sentence: He gave a guarded response to the reporter's question.

guffaw.def: a loud, hearty burst of laughter.
guffaw.sentence: His joke elicited a guffaw from the entire audience.

guile.sentence: She used her guile to negotiate a better deal.

guileless.def: Free from cunning or deceit; innocent.
guileless.sentence: His guileless smile won everyone's trust.

guise.def: An external appearance, often concealing the true nature.
guise.sentence: The spy entered the building under the guise of a journalist.

gullible.def: Easily persuaded to believe something; credulous.
gullible.sentence: The gullible tourist fell for the scam.

gumption.sentence: She had the gumption to start her own business.

gustatory.def: Relating to the sense of taste.
gustatory.sentence: The chef's gustatory creations delighted the diners.

gusto.def: Enthusiasm and energy in doing something.

guttural.sentence: The language is known for its guttural consonants.

guy.def: A man; also a rope or cable used to steady something.

habitat.def: The natural home or environment of an organism.
habitat.sentence: The forest is the habitat of many birds.

hackneyed.sentence: The plot of the movie was hackneyed.

haggard.def: Looking exhausted and unwell, from worry or lack of sleep.
haggard.sentence: After the long journey, he looked haggard.

hagiographic.sentence: The biography was hagiographic, ignoring his flaws.

hail.def: To greet or acclaim enthusiastically; also to fall as hail.

halcyon.def: Denoting a period of time that is idyllically happy and peaceful.
halcyon.sentence: She recalled the halcyon days of her youth.

hallmark.sentence: Precision is the hallmark of his work.

hallow.def: To make holy; to honor as holy.
hallow.sentence: The ground was hallowed by the ceremony.

hallowed.sentence: The hallowed halls of the university.

hallucinate.def: To experience a perception of something not present.
hallucinate.sentence: He began to hallucinate after taking the drug.

hamper.def: To hinder or impede the movement or progress of.

hamstrung.sentence: The project was hamstrung by lack of funds.

hand-wringing.def: Expression of distress or worry, often excessive.
hand-wringing.sentence: There was much hand-wringing over the decision.

handwringing.sentence: The handwringing continued despite the positive outcome.

hankering.sentence: She had a hankering for chocolate.

haphazard.def: Lacking any obvious principle of organization; random.
haphazard.sentence: The books were arranged in a haphazard manner.

hapless.sentence: The hapless traveler lost his luggage and missed his flight.

haptic.def: Relating to the sense of touch.
haptic.sentence: The haptic feedback on the smartphone simulates the feeling of pressing a button.

harangue.sentence: The coach delivered a harangue to the team after their poor performance.

harass.def: To subject to aggressive pressure or intimidation.
harass.sentence: The bully would harass the younger students every day.

harbinger.def: A person or thing that announces or signals the approach of another.
harbinger.sentence: The robin is often considered a harbinger of spring.

harbor.def: To give shelter or refuge; to hold a thought or feeling secretly.
harbor.sentence: She harbored a deep resentment toward her former friend.

hardscrabble.def: Involving or requiring great effort; difficult and unrewarding.
hardscrabble.sentence: The farmer led a hardscrabble existence on the rocky soil.

hardy.def: Robust; capable of enduring difficult conditions.
hardy.sentence: Hardy plants can survive the harsh winter frost.

harmonious.def: Forming a pleasing or consistent whole; free from disagreement.
harmonious.sentence: The choir produced a harmonious blend of voices.

harried.def: Feeling strained as a result of having too many demands.
harried.sentence: The harried mother rushed to pick up her children from school.

harrow.sentence: The tragic news harrowed the entire community.

harrowing.sentence: The survivors told harrowing tales of their ordeal.

haughty.sentence: The haughty princess looked down on the servants.

hauteur.def: Haughtiness in bearing and attitude; arrogance.
hauteur.sentence: His hauteur made him unpopular among his peers.

have the upper hand.def: To have a position of advantage or control.
have the upper hand.sentence: After the negotiation, the company had the upper hand.

haven.def: A place of safety or refuge.
haven.sentence: The library was a quiet haven for the book lovers.

havoc.sentence: The storm wreaked havoc on the coastal towns.

headlong.def: With great haste; without careful thought.
headlong.sentence: He rushed headlong into the dangerous situation.

headstrong.def: Determined to have one's own way; willful.
headstrong.sentence: The headstrong child refused to listen to his parents.

heady.def: Having a strong or exhilarating effect; intoxicating.
heady.sentence: The heady scent of roses filled the garden.

hearken.def: To listen attentively; to give heed.
hearken.sentence: Hearken to the wise words of your elders.

hector.def: To talk to someone in a bullying way.
hector.sentence: The boss would hector his employees over minor mistakes.

hectoring.sentence: His hectoring tone made everyone uncomfortable.

hedge.def: To avoid making a definite statement or commitment.
hedge.sentence: When asked about his plans, he hedged and changed the subject.

hedonism.def: The pursuit of pleasure as the highest good.
hedonism.sentence: The philosophy of hedonism emphasizes immediate gratification.

hedonist.def: A person who believes that pleasure is the most important thing in life.
hedonist.sentence: As a dedicated hedonist, she spent her weekends indulging in gourmet meals and spa treatments.

heed.def: To pay attention to; take notice of.
heed.sentence: The driver failed to heed the warning signs and drove into the flooded road.

hegemony.def: Leadership or dominance, especially by one country or social group over others.
hegemony.sentence: The region's economic hegemony was challenged by the rise of new industrial powers.

heinous.sentence: The heinous crime shocked the entire community.

herald.def: To be a sign that something is about to happen.
herald.sentence: The first robin heralds the arrival of spring.

herbaceous.def: Relating to herbs; having the characteristics of a herb.
herbaceous.sentence: The garden was filled with herbaceous plants that died back each winter.

herbivorous.sentence: Cows are herbivorous animals that graze on grass.

heresy.def: Belief or opinion contrary to orthodox religious doctrine.
heresy.sentence: In the Middle Ages, questioning church teachings was considered heresy.

heretic.def: A person holding an opinion at odds with what is generally accepted.
heretic.sentence: Galileo was declared a heretic for supporting heliocentrism.

heretical.def: Holding an opinion at odds with what is generally accepted.
heretical.sentence: His heretical views on economics were dismissed by mainstream scholars.

hermetic.def: Completely sealed, especially against the escape or entry of air.
hermetic.sentence: The laboratory samples were stored in hermetic containers to prevent contamination.

heterodox.def: Not conforming with accepted or orthodox standards.
heterodox.sentence: The professor's heterodox theories on evolution sparked heated debate.

heterogeneous.sentence: The city's population is heterogeneous, comprising many ethnic groups.

hew.def: To chop or cut with an axe, pick, or other tool.
hew.sentence: The lumberjack hewed the tree into logs.

heyday.def: The period of a person's or thing's greatest success or popularity.
heyday.sentence: In its heyday, the company was the world's leading manufacturer of typewriters.

hiatus.def: A pause or gap in a sequence, series, or process.
hiatus.sentence: After a two-year hiatus, the band released a new album.

hidebound.def: Unwilling to change because of tradition or convention.
hidebound.sentence: The hidebound committee refused to consider any new proposals.

hierarchical.def: Arranged in order of rank or importance.
hierarchical.sentence: The company's hierarchical structure made it difficult for lower-level employees to share ideas.

hierarchy.def: A system in which members are ranked according to relative status or authority.
hierarchy.sentence: In the military hierarchy, a general outranks a colonel.

hieroglyphics.def: A system of writing using pictorial symbols, especially used in ancient Egypt.
hieroglyphics.sentence: The archaeologist spent years deciphering the hieroglyphics on the temple walls.

hinder.def: To create difficulties that delay or prevent progress.
hinder.sentence: The heavy rain hindered the rescue efforts.

hirsute.sentence: The hirsute man had a thick beard and bushy eyebrows.

histrionic.def: Overly theatrical or melodramatic in character or style.
histrionic.sentence: Her histrionic reaction to the minor accident drew everyone's attention.

histrionics.def: Melodramatic behavior designed to attract attention.
histrionics.sentence: His histrionics during the meeting annoyed his colleagues.

hoard.def: To accumulate and store a large supply of something, often secretly.
hoard.sentence: During the shortage, some people hoarded toilet paper.

hoary.def: Very old or old-fashioned; gray or white with age.
hoary.sentence: The hoary old professor told stories from his youth.

hoax.sentence: The email about winning a lottery was a hoax.

hobble.def: To walk awkwardly or with difficulty; to impede or restrict.
hobble.sentence: The injured runner could only hobble to the finish line.

Hobson's choice.def: A choice that is really no choice at all, as only one option is offered.
Hobson's choice.sentence: Faced with Hobson's choice, he accepted the job offer.

hodgepodge.sentence: The essay was a hodgepodge of unrelated ideas.

holistic.def: Characterized by the belief that the parts of something are interconnected and can be understood only by reference to the whole.
holistic.sentence: She takes a holistic approach to health, considering diet, exercise, and mental well-being.

holster.def: A holder for a handgun, typically worn on a belt or shoulder strap.
holster.sentence: The detective drew his gun from the holster.

homage.def: Special honor or respect shown publicly.
homage.sentence: The concert paid homage to the legendary musician.

homeopathy.def: A system of alternative medicine based on treating like with like using highly diluted substances.
homeopathy.sentence: She prefers homeopathy over conventional medicine for minor ailments.

homeostasis.def: The tendency of a system, especially the body, to maintain internal stability.
homeostasis.sentence: The body's homeostasis regulates temperature and pH levels.

homespun.def: Simple and unsophisticated; made at home.
homespun.sentence: Her homespun wisdom came from years of experience.

homiletics.def: The art of preaching or writing sermons.
homiletics.sentence: The seminary offers a course in homiletics.

homily.def: A short sermon or moralizing lecture.
homily.sentence: The priest delivered a homily on forgiveness.

homogeneous.def: Of the same kind; uniform in composition or character.
homogeneous.sentence: The population was remarkably homogeneous, with little diversity.

homogenous.def: Variant spelling of homogeneous; of the same kind.
homogenous.sentence: The group was homogenous in its political views.

hoodwink.sentence: The con artist hoodwinked the elderly couple out of their savings.

hook, line, and sinker.sentence: He fell for the scam hook, line, and sinker.

hortatory.def: Encouraging or urging to some action; exhorting.
hortatory.sentence: The hortatory speech motivated the team to work harder.

hostile.sentence: The hostile crowd booed the speaker.

hotly.def: In an angry, passionate, or intense manner.
hotly.sentence: The two candidates hotly debated the issue.

hound.sentence: The paparazzi hounded the celebrity everywhere she went.

hubris.sentence: His hubris led to his downfall.

humdrum.sentence: She was tired of her humdrum routine.

humility.def: A modest or low view of one's own importance; humbleness.
humility.sentence: He accepted the award with humility.

husband.sentence: She husbanded her energy for the final sprint.

husbandry.def: The management of resources, especially in farming or animal care.
husbandry.sentence: Good husbandry practices ensure the farm remains productive for generations.

hyperbole.def: Exaggerated statements not meant to be taken literally.
hyperbole.sentence: Saying 'I've told you a million times' is a common hyperbole.

hypocritical.def: Behaving in a way that suggests one has higher standards than is the case.
hypocritical.sentence: It's hypocritical to criticize others for littering when you do it yourself.

hysterical.def: Deriving from or affected by uncontrolled extreme emotion.
hysterical.sentence: The crowd became hysterical with excitement when the band appeared.

I'm from Missouri.def: A phrase indicating skepticism and a need for proof.
I'm from Missouri.sentence: He claimed he could run a mile in four minutes, but I'm from Missouri—show me.

iconoclast.def: A person who attacks cherished beliefs or institutions.
iconoclast.sentence: The artist was an iconoclast, challenging traditional notions of beauty.

iconoclastic.def: Criticizing or attacking cherished beliefs or institutions.
iconoclastic.sentence: Her iconoclastic views often sparked heated debates.

ideological.def: Relating to a system of ideas and ideals, especially concerning politics or economics.
ideological.sentence: The debate was more ideological than practical.

idiosyncrasy.def: A distinctive or peculiar feature or characteristic of a person or thing.
idiosyncrasy.sentence: One of his idiosyncrasies was wearing mismatched socks.

idiosyncratic.sentence: Her idiosyncratic style made her stand out in the fashion world.

idolatrous.def: Relating to or practicing idolatry; blindly adoring.
idolatrous.sentence: The fans' idolatrous devotion to the singer bordered on obsession.

idolatry.def: Extreme admiration, love, or reverence for something or someone.
idolatry.sentence: The cult's idolatry of their leader was alarming.

idyll.def: An extremely happy, peaceful, or picturesque episode or scene.
idyll.sentence: Their life on the farm was an idyll of simplicity.

idyllic.sentence: They spent an idyllic week at the beach.

igneous.def: Relating to or denoting rocks formed by solidification of molten magma.
igneous.sentence: Granite is an igneous rock formed from cooled magma.

ignoble.def: Not honorable in character or purpose; dishonorable.
ignoble.sentence: His ignoble motives were soon revealed.

ignominious.def: Deserving or causing public disgrace or shame.
ignominious.sentence: The team suffered an ignominious defeat.

ignominy.sentence: He resigned in ignominy after the scandal.

ilk.def: A type or kind (used to refer to a group of people or things).
ilk.sentence: I don't trust politicians of that ilk.

illiberal.def: Opposed to liberal principles; restricting freedom of thought or behavior.
illiberal.sentence: The regime's illiberal policies suppressed dissent.

illicit.def: Forbidden by law, rules, or custom.
illicit.sentence: The police cracked down on illicit drug trade.

illusory.sentence: The promise of quick wealth proved illusory.

illustrious.def: Well known, respected, and admired for past achievements.
illustrious.sentence: She had an illustrious career as a diplomat.

imbibe.def: To drink (alcohol); to absorb or assimilate ideas or knowledge.
imbibe.sentence: He liked to imbibe a glass of wine with dinner.

imbroglio.def: An extremely confused, complicated, or embarrassing situation.
imbroglio.sentence: The political imbroglio led to a government shutdown.

imbue.def: to inspire or permeate with a feeling or quality
imbue.sentence: The teacher imbued her students with a love of learning.

immaculate.sentence: Her immaculate white dress was spotless after the party.

immaterial.sentence: The color of the car is immaterial to its performance.

imminent.sentence: With the dark clouds gathering, a storm seemed imminent.

immutable.def: unchanging over time or unable to be changed
immutable.sentence: The laws of physics are immutable.

impair.sentence: Loud noise can impair your hearing over time.

impartial.def: treating all rivals or disputants equally; fair and just
impartial.sentence: A judge must be impartial in the courtroom.

impasse.def: a situation in which no progress is possible
impasse.sentence: The negotiations reached an impasse when neither side would compromise.

impassive.sentence: Despite the shocking news, she remained impassive.

impeccable.def: in accordance with the highest standards; faultless
impeccable.sentence: Her impeccable manners impressed everyone at the dinner.

impecunious.sentence: The impecunious student could barely afford textbooks.

impede.def: to delay or block the progress of
impede.sentence: The heavy snow impeded traffic on the highway.

impeded.sentence: The rescue efforts were impeded by the dense fog.

impediment.sentence: His lack of experience was an impediment to getting the job.

impending.sentence: The impending deadline caused a lot of stress.

impenetrable.def: impossible to pass through or enter

imperative.sentence: It is imperative that you arrive on time.

imperceptible.sentence: The difference in color was imperceptible to the naked eye.

imperious.def: assuming power or authority without justification; arrogant and domineering
imperious.sentence: Her imperious tone made everyone feel uncomfortable.

impermeable.def: not allowing fluid to pass through
impermeable.sentence: The raincoat is made of impermeable fabric.

impertinent.sentence: The student's impertinent remark got him sent to the principal's office.

imperturbable.def: unable to be upset or excited; calm
imperturbable.sentence: Even in a crisis, she remained imperturbable.

impervious.def: not allowing fluid to pass through; not affected by something

impetuous.def: acting or done quickly and without thought or care
impetuous.sentence: His impetuous decision to quit his job surprised everyone.

impetus.def: the force or energy with which a body moves; something that makes a process happen
impetus.sentence: The new policy gave impetus to economic growth.

impiety.def: Lack of reverence or respect for God or sacred things.
impiety.sentence: The priest condemned the widespread impiety of the modern age.

impinge.def: To have an effect or impact, especially a negative one.
impinge.sentence: The new regulations impinge on our freedom to operate.

impious.def: Showing a lack of respect for God or religion.
impious.sentence: His impious remarks offended the congregation.

implacable.def: Unable to be appeased or pacified.
implacable.sentence: The enemy was implacable and refused all offers of peace.

implausible.def: Not seeming reasonable or probable; failing to convince.
implausible.sentence: The alibi seemed implausible to the detective.

implicate.def: To show someone to be involved in a crime or wrongdoing.
implicate.sentence: The evidence implicated several high-ranking officials.

implication.def: A conclusion that can be drawn from something, though not explicitly stated.
implication.sentence: The implication of his statement was that we were not welcome.

implicit.sentence: There was an implicit understanding between them.

implode.def: To collapse or burst inward violently.
implode.sentence: The building was demolished in a controlled implosion.

implore.def: To beg someone earnestly or desperately to do something.

imploring.def: Expressing a desperate appeal or entreaty.
imploring.sentence: He gave her an imploring look.

imponderable.def: Difficult or impossible to estimate, assess, or answer.
imponderable.sentence: The long-term effects of the policy are imponderable.

importune.def: To harass with persistent requests or demands.
importune.sentence: The beggar importuned passersby for money.

importuned.def: Past tense of importune; harassed with persistent requests.
importuned.sentence: She importuned her parents for a new phone.

imposture.def: An instance of pretending to be someone else in order to deceive.
imposture.sentence: The spy's imposture was eventually discovered.

imprecation.sentence: He muttered imprecations under his breath.

impregnable.def: Unable to be captured or broken into.

impresario.def: A person who organizes and often finances concerts, plays, or operas.
impresario.sentence: The impresario launched a new season of opera.

imprimatur.def: Official approval or sanction, especially from a church authority.
imprimatur.sentence: The book was published with the imprimatur of the Vatican.

improbable.def: Not likely to be true or to happen.
improbable.sentence: It is improbable that she will arrive on time.

impromptu.def: Done without being planned or rehearsed.

improvident.def: Not having or showing foresight; spending money carelessly.
improvident.sentence: His improvident spending left him in debt.

improvise.def: To create and perform spontaneously without preparation.
improvise.sentence: The chef had to improvise with what was in the fridge.

imprudence.sentence: The imprudence of the decision led to disaster.

imprudent.def: Not showing care for the consequences; unwise.
imprudent.sentence: It would be imprudent to invest all your money in one stock.

impudence.sentence: The student's impudence earned him a detention.

impudent.sentence: The impudent child talked back to the teacher.

impugn.def: To dispute the truth, validity, or honesty of (a statement or motive); call into question.
impugn.sentence: The lawyer impugned the witness's testimony.

impunity.def: Exemption from punishment or freedom from the injurious consequences of an action.
impunity.sentence: The corrupt official acted with impunity for years.

impute.def: To attribute or ascribe (something, especially a fault or crime) to someone.
impute.sentence: They imputed the accident to driver error.

in a bee line.def: In a straight, direct line; without deviation.
in a bee line.sentence: He headed in a bee line for the exit.

in apple pie order.sentence: She kept her desk in apple pie order.

in seventh heaven.def: In a state of extreme happiness or bliss.
in seventh heaven.sentence: She was in seventh heaven after winning the lottery.

in the arms of Morpheus.sentence: He was in the arms of Morpheus by midnight.

in the doldrums.def: In a state of depression or inactivity; stagnant.
in the doldrums.sentence: The economy has been in the doldrums for months.

in the lap of the gods.def: Beyond human control; dependent on fate.
in the lap of the gods.sentence: The outcome of the election is in the lap of the gods.

inadvertent.def: Not resulting from or achieved through deliberate planning; unintentional.
inadvertent.sentence: The error was inadvertent and quickly corrected.

inadvertently.sentence: He inadvertently deleted the important file.

inalienable.def: Unable to be taken away from or given away by the possessor.
inalienable.sentence: Freedom of speech is an inalienable right.

inane.sentence: The movie's plot was inane and predictable.

inanimate.def: Not alive, especially not in the manner of animals and humans; lifeless.
inanimate.sentence: The room was filled with inanimate objects.

inanity.def: Lack of sense or meaning; silliness.
inanity.sentence: The inanity of the conversation bored everyone.

inarticulate.def: Unable to speak distinctly or express oneself clearly.
inarticulate.sentence: He was so nervous he became inarticulate.

inasmuch.def: To the extent that; insofar as.
inasmuch.sentence: Inasmuch as you are my friend, I will help you.

inauspicious.def: Unfavorable; unpromising; showing signs of a negative outcome.
inauspicious.sentence: The project got off to an inauspicious start.

inborn.sentence: She has an inborn talent for music.

incandescent.def: Emitting light as a result of being heated; also, extremely angry.
incandescent.sentence: The incandescent bulb lit the room, and the boss was incandescent with rage.

incapacitated.def: Deprived of strength or ability; disabled.
incapacitated.sentence: The accident left him incapacitated for weeks.

incendiary.def: Tending to stir up conflict; inflammatory; also, designed to cause fires.
incendiary.sentence: The politician's incendiary remarks sparked protests.

incense.def: a substance that produces a fragrant odor when burned
incense.sentence: The room was filled with the sweet smell of burning incense.

incensed.sentence: She was incensed by the unfair treatment.

incentive.def: something that motivates or encourages someone to do something
incentive.sentence: The bonus served as an incentive for employees to work harder.

incessant.sentence: The incessant noise from the construction site gave me a headache.

inchoate.def: just begun and not fully formed or developed; rudimentary
inchoate.sentence: The plan was still inchoate and needed more thought.

incidence.def: the occurrence, rate, or frequency of something
incidence.sentence: The incidence of flu increases during winter.

incidental.def: happening as a minor part of something else; secondary
incidental.sentence: The cost of food was incidental to the overall trip expenses.

incipient.sentence: The incipient signs of the disease were easy to miss.

incisive.sentence: Her incisive comments cut through the confusion.

inclement.sentence: The inclement weather forced the cancellation of the outdoor event.

inclined.def: tending or leaning toward a particular behavior or opinion
inclined.sentence: He was inclined to believe her story.

incoherent.def: unable to speak clearly or logically
incoherent.sentence: The patient was incoherent after the accident.

incompatibility.def: the condition of not being able to exist or work together
incompatibility.sentence: The incompatibility of their personalities led to frequent arguments.

inconclusive.def: not leading to a definite conclusion or result
inconclusive.sentence: The evidence was inconclusive, so the case remained open.

incongruity.def: the state of being out of place or inconsistent
incongruity.sentence: The incongruity of his formal attire at the beach party was amusing.

incongruous.def: not in harmony or keeping with the surroundings; out of place
incongruous.sentence: The modern building looked incongruous among the historic houses.

inconsequential.sentence: The error was inconsequential and did not affect the outcome.

inconstancy.def: the quality of being changeable and fickle
inconstancy.sentence: Her inconstancy made it hard to trust her promises.

incontrovertible.def: not able to be denied or disputed

incorporate.def: to include or integrate as part of a whole
incorporate.sentence: We will incorporate your feedback into the final design.

incorrigible.def: not able to be corrected, improved, or reformed
incorrigible.sentence: The incorrigible child refused to follow any rules.

incredulous.def: unwilling or unable to believe something
incredulous.sentence: She gave him an incredulous look when he claimed to have seen a UFO.

incubation.def: the process of keeping eggs warm to hatch them, or the development of an infection before symptoms appear
incubation.sentence: The incubation period for the virus is about two weeks.

inculcate.def: to instill an idea, attitude, or habit by persistent instruction
inculcate.sentence: Parents should inculcate good manners in their children.

incumbent.def: necessary for someone as a duty or responsibility
incumbent.sentence: It is incumbent upon the manager to ensure safety.

incursion.def: A sudden invasion or attack, especially a brief one.
incursion.sentence: The military launched a swift incursion into enemy territory.

indebted.def: Owing gratitude or recognition to another for help or favor.
indebted.sentence: I am deeply indebted to my mentor for her guidance.

indecorous.def: Lacking propriety or good taste; unseemly.
indecorous.sentence: His indecorous behavior at the formal dinner shocked the guests.

indefatigable.def: Persisting tirelessly; never showing signs of fatigue.
indefatigable.sentence: The indefatigable researcher worked late into the night.

indelible.def: Making marks that cannot be removed; unforgettable.
indelible.sentence: The experience left an indelible impression on her.

indeterminate.def: Not precisely determined or fixed; vague.

Indian summer.def: A period of unseasonably warm weather in late autumn.
Indian summer.sentence: We enjoyed an Indian summer with sunny days in October.

indict.def: To formally accuse of or charge with a serious crime.
indict.sentence: The grand jury decided to indict the suspect for fraud.

indictment.def: A formal charge or accusation of a serious crime.
indictment.sentence: The indictment listed multiple counts of embezzlement.

indifference.def: Lack of interest, concern, or sympathy.
indifference.sentence: She viewed the crisis with complete indifference.

indifferent.def: Having no particular interest or sympathy; unconcerned.
indifferent.sentence: He was indifferent to the outcome of the game.

indigence.def: A state of extreme poverty; destitution.
indigence.sentence: The charity works to alleviate indigence in rural areas.

indigenous.def: Originating naturally in a particular place; native.
indigenous.sentence: The indigenous people have lived here for centuries.

indigent.sentence: The program provides free meals for indigent families.

indignant.def: Feeling or showing anger at unfair treatment.
indignant.sentence: She was indignant at the false accusations.

indignation.def: Anger provoked by unfairness or injustice.
indignation.sentence: The crowd expressed indignation at the verdict.

indiscriminate.def: Done without careful judgment; random or haphazard.
indiscriminate.sentence: The indiscriminate use of antibiotics leads to resistance.

indispensable.sentence: A good dictionary is indispensable for learning a language.

indoctrinate.def: To teach a person or group to accept a set of beliefs uncritically.
indoctrinate.sentence: The regime sought to indoctrinate children with its ideology.

indolent.def: Wanting to avoid activity or exertion; lazy.
indolent.sentence: The indolent student rarely completed assignments on time.

indomitable.def: Impossible to subdue or defeat; unconquerable.
indomitable.sentence: Her indomitable spirit helped her overcome many obstacles.

induce.def: To persuade or influence someone to do something.
induce.sentence: The advertisement induced many people to buy the product.

indulge.def: To allow oneself to enjoy something desired.
indulge.sentence: On vacation, she decided to indulge in a spa treatment.

industrious.sentence: The industrious worker always completed tasks ahead of schedule.

inebriated.sentence: He was too inebriated to drive home safely.

ineffable.def: Too great or extreme to be expressed or described in words.
ineffable.sentence: The beauty of the sunset was ineffable, leaving the onlookers speechless.

ineffectual.def: Not producing the desired effect; ineffective.
ineffectual.sentence: The ineffectual attempts to fix the leak only made it worse.

ineluctable.def: Unable to be resisted or avoided; inescapable.
ineluctable.sentence: The ineluctable march of time brings change to all things.

inept.def: Having or showing no skill; clumsy or incompetent.
inept.sentence: His inept handling of the situation led to a public relations disaster.

inequity.def: Lack of fairness or justice; partiality.
inequity.sentence: The inequity in the distribution of resources sparked widespread protests.

inerrancy.def: The quality of being incapable of error; infallibility.
inerrancy.sentence: The doctrine of biblical inerrancy is central to some religious traditions.

inert.def: Lacking the ability or strength to move; inactive or sluggish.
inert.sentence: The inert gas filled the chamber to prevent any chemical reaction.

inestimable.def: Too great to be calculated or measured; invaluable.
inestimable.sentence: The support of her family was of inestimable value during the crisis.

inevitable.sentence: Given the heavy rain, flooding was inevitable.

inexorable.def: Impossible to stop or prevent; unyielding.
inexorable.sentence: The inexorable advance of technology changes every industry.

inextricable.def: Impossible to untangle or separate; closely linked.
inextricable.sentence: The history of the two countries is inextricable.

infallible.def: Incapable of making mistakes or being wrong.
infallible.sentence: No leader is infallible; everyone makes errors.

infamous.def: Well known for some bad or evil quality or deed.
infamous.sentence: The infamous pirate Blackbeard terrorized the Caribbean.

infer.def: To deduce or conclude from evidence and reasoning.
infer.sentence: From the footprints, we can infer that someone entered the house.

infiltrate.def: To enter or gain access to (an organization, place, etc.) surreptitiously and gradually.
infiltrate.sentence: The spy managed to infiltrate the enemy headquarters.

infinitesimal.def: Extremely small; so small as to be almost negligible.
infinitesimal.sentence: The chance of winning the lottery is infinitesimal.

inflammable.sentence: Store inflammable materials away from heat sources.

inflicted.def: Caused (something unpleasant or painful) to be suffered by someone or something.
inflicted.sentence: The storm inflicted severe damage on the coastal town.

influx.def: An arrival or entry of large numbers of people or things.
influx.sentence: The city experienced an influx of tourists during the festival.

inform.sentence: Please inform us of any changes to your schedule.

infraction.def: A violation or infringement of a law, rule, or agreement.
infraction.sentence: Parking in a handicapped spot without a permit is a minor infraction.

infuse.def: To fill or cause to be filled with something (such as a quality or feeling).
infuse.sentence: The teacher infused the lesson with enthusiasm.

ingenious.sentence: She devised an ingenious solution to the problem.

ingenue.def: An innocent or unsophisticated young woman, especially in a play or film.
ingenue.sentence: The actress was typecast as the ingenue in romantic comedies.

ingenuity.def: The quality of being clever, original, and inventive.
ingenuity.sentence: The engineer's ingenuity saved the company millions.

ingenuous.def: Showing innocent or childlike simplicity and candidness.
ingenuous.sentence: Her ingenuous smile made everyone trust her immediately.

ingrained.def: Firmly established and difficult to change, often due to long existence.
ingrained.sentence: His ingrained habits were hard to break.

ingrate.sentence: After all I did for him, he turned out to be an ingrate.

ingratiate.def: To bring oneself into favor with someone by flattery or doing favors.
ingratiate.sentence: He tried to ingratiate himself with the boss by complimenting her work.

inherent.def: Existing in something as a permanent, essential characteristic.
inherent.sentence: The risks are inherent in the job.

inhibit.def: To hinder, restrain, or prevent an action or process.
inhibit.sentence: The medication can inhibit the growth of bacteria.

inhibition.def: A feeling that makes one self-conscious and unable to act naturally.
inhibition.sentence: He lost his inhibition after a few drinks.

inimical.def: Tending to obstruct or harm; unfriendly or hostile.
inimical.sentence: The policy was inimical to economic growth.

inimitable.def: So good or unusual as to be impossible to copy; unique.
inimitable.sentence: She has an inimitable style of singing.

iniquitous.sentence: The iniquitous system oppressed the poor.

iniquity.sentence: The novel exposes the iniquity of the regime.

initiate.def: To cause a process or action to begin.
initiate.sentence: The company will initiate a new marketing campaign.

inkling.def: A slight knowledge or suspicion; a hint.
inkling.sentence: I had an inkling that something was wrong.

innate.sentence: Birds have an innate ability to fly.

innervate.def: To supply an organ or body part with nerves or stimulate it.
innervate.sentence: The nerve fibers innervate the muscles.

innocuous.sentence: The snake is innocuous to humans.

Innocuous.sentence: The snake is innocuous to humans.

inopportune.def: Occurring at an inconvenient or inappropriate time.
inopportune.sentence: The phone call came at an inopportune moment.

inordinate.sentence: She spent an inordinate amount of time on the project.
