ostensible.sentence: His ostensible reason for leaving was a family emergency, but no one believed him.

ostensible or ostensive.sentence: The ostensive definition pointed to the object itself.

ostentatious.def: characterized by vulgar or pretentious display; designed to impress or attract notice.
ostentatious.sentence: The billionaire's ostentatious mansion was visible from miles away.

ostracism.def: exclusion from a group or society by general consent.
ostracism.sentence: He faced social ostracism after speaking out against the community's traditions.

ostracize.def: to exclude someone from a group or society.
ostracize.sentence: The students ostracized the new kid for wearing unusual clothes.

ostracized.def: past tense of ostracize; excluded from a group.
ostracized.sentence: After the scandal, she was ostracized by her former friends.

otiose.sentence: His otiose comments only prolonged the meeting.

oust.def: to drive out or expel someone from a position or place.
oust.sentence: The rebels ousted the dictator in a swift coup.

out of one's depth.def: in a situation that is too difficult for one to handle.
out of one's depth.sentence: When the conversation turned to quantum physics, I was out of my depth.

out of the frying pan into the fire.def: from a bad situation to a worse one.
out of the frying pan into the fire.sentence: He left his stressful job only to start a business that failed, going out of the frying pan into the fire.

out on a limb.def: in a vulnerable or isolated position, often by taking a risk.
out on a limb.sentence: She went out on a limb to defend her colleague's controversial idea.

outgrowth.def: a natural development or result of something.
outgrowth.sentence: The new policy was an outgrowth of years of research.

outlandish.def: looking or sounding bizarre or unfamiliar.
outlandish.sentence: His outlandish outfit drew stares from everyone in the room.

outmoded.def: no longer in fashion or use; outdated.
outmoded.sentence: The factory's outmoded equipment was replaced with modern machinery.

outset.def: the start or beginning of something.
outset.sentence: From the outset, the project was plagued with problems.

outstrip.def: to exceed or surpass in speed, quantity, or performance.
outstrip.sentence: Demand for the new product outstripped supply within days.

overhaul.def: to take apart and examine thoroughly in order to repair or improve.
overhaul.sentence: The mechanic overhauled the engine, replacing all worn parts.

overshadow.def: to cast a shadow over; to make less important or noticeable.
overshadow.sentence: The star player's injury overshadowed the team's victory.

overt.def: done or shown openly; not secret or hidden.
overt.sentence: The government's overt support for the policy surprised many.

overture.def: an opening move or proposal indicating a willingness to negotiate or begin a relationship.
overture.sentence: The peace overture was rejected by the rebels.

overweening.def: showing excessive confidence or pride; arrogant.
overweening.sentence: His overweening ambition led him to make reckless decisions.

overwrought.def: in a state of nervous excitement or anxiety; overdone.
overwrought.sentence: She was overwrought with worry about the exam results.

pacific.def: peaceful in character or intent; tending to lessen conflict.
pacific.sentence: The pacific protest was a stark contrast to the violent riots elsewhere.

pacify.def: to quell the anger, agitation, or excitement of; to restore peace.
pacify.sentence: The mother pacified the crying baby with a lullaby.

paean.def: a song of praise, triumph, or thanksgiving
paean.sentence: The crowd sang a paean to celebrate the team's victory.

painstaking.def: done with great care and thoroughness
painstaking.sentence: The artist's painstaking attention to detail made the portrait lifelike.

palatable.def: pleasant to taste; acceptable or satisfactory
palatable.sentence: The chef made the dish palatable even for picky eaters.

palatial.def: resembling a palace in being spacious and splendid
palatial.sentence: The billionaire's mansion was palatial, with marble floors and gold fixtures.

palaver.sentence: The meeting was mostly palaver, with no decisions made.

palimpsest.def: a manuscript or piece of writing material on which later writing has been superimposed on earlier writing
palimpsest.sentence: The ancient palimpsest revealed layers of text from different centuries.

pall.def: become less interesting or enjoyable over time
pall.sentence: The novelty of the game began to pall after several hours.

palliate.def: make (a disease or its symptoms) less severe without removing the cause
palliate.sentence: The medication helped palliate the patient's pain.

pallid.def: pale, typically because of poor health
pallid.sentence: After the illness, her face looked pallid and drawn.

palpable.def: able to be touched or felt; easily perceived
palpable.sentence: The tension in the room was palpable as the verdict was read.

paltry.def: small or meager in amount; insignificant
paltry.sentence: He was paid a paltry sum for his hard work.

pan.sentence: The critics panned the movie for its weak plot.

panacea.def: a solution or remedy for all difficulties or diseases
panacea.sentence: There is no panacea for the economic problems facing the country.

panache.def: flamboyant confidence of style or manner
panache.sentence: She performed the dance with great panache.

pandemic.def: a widespread occurrence of an infectious disease over a whole country or the world
pandemic.sentence: The COVID-19 pandemic affected millions of people globally.

pandemonium.def: wild and noisy disorder or confusion; uproar
pandemonium.sentence: When the fire alarm went off, there was pandemonium in the building.

pander.def: gratify or indulge (an immoral or distasteful desire, need, or habit)
pander.sentence: The politician pandered to the crowd's prejudices.

panegyric.def: a public speech or published text in praise of someone or something
panegyric.sentence: The eulogy was a heartfelt panegyric to the deceased.

pangs.def: a sudden sharp pain or painful emotion
pangs.sentence: She felt pangs of guilt after lying to her friend.

panned.sentence: The film was panned by reviewers for its poor acting.

panoply.sentence: The parade featured a panoply of colorful costumes.

parable.def: a simple story used to illustrate a moral or spiritual lesson
parable.sentence: Jesus often taught using parables like the Good Samaritan.

paradigm.def: a typical example or pattern of something; a model
paradigm.sentence: His work became a paradigm for future researchers.

paradigmatic.def: serving as a typical example of something
paradigmatic.sentence: The case is paradigmatic of the challenges faced by small businesses.

paradox.def: a seemingly absurd or self-contradictory statement that may be true
paradox.sentence: The paradox of 'less is more' is often true in design.

paradoxical.sentence: It is paradoxical that standing still can sometimes help you move forward.

paragon.def: A model of excellence or perfection.
paragon.sentence: She was considered a paragon of virtue in her community.

paramount.def: More important than anything else; supreme.
paramount.sentence: Safety is of paramount importance in this experiment.

parasite.def: An organism that lives on or in another and benefits at its expense.
parasite.sentence: The tick is a parasite that feeds on the blood of its host.

pare.def: Trim by cutting away the outer edges or skin.
pare.sentence: She used a knife to pare the apple.

parenthetical.def: Relating to or inserted as a parenthesis.
parenthetical.sentence: He added a parenthetical remark to clarify his point.

pariah.def: An outcast or someone rejected by society.
pariah.sentence: After the scandal, he became a pariah in his social circle.

parley.def: A discussion or conference between opposing sides.
parley.sentence: The two leaders agreed to a parley to negotiate a truce.

parochial.def: Having a limited or narrow outlook; provincial.
parochial.sentence: His parochial views prevented him from appreciating other cultures.

parody.def: An imitation of a style for comic effect or ridicule.
parody.sentence: The comedian's parody of the politician was hilarious.

paroxysm.def: A sudden outburst of emotion or action.
paroxysm.sentence: He burst into a paroxysm of laughter.

parquetry.sentence: The floor was decorated with intricate parquetry.

parry.def: To ward off or deflect a blow or question.
parry.sentence: He skillfully parried the reporter's probing questions.

parsimonious.sentence: The parsimonious millionaire refused to donate to charity.

partial.sentence: The judge was accused of being partial to the defendant.

partiality.def: A bias or preference for something.
partiality.sentence: Her partiality for chocolate was well known.

partisan.def: A strong supporter of a party or cause.
partisan.sentence: The partisan refused to listen to opposing views.

parvenu.def: A person who has recently gained wealth but lacks social refinement.
parvenu.sentence: The parvenu tried to impress guests with his expensive art collection.

pass the buck.sentence: Instead of solving the problem, he tried to pass the buck.

passe.def: No longer fashionable; out of date.
passe.sentence: That hairstyle is now considered passe.

pastiche.def: An artistic work imitating various styles.
pastiche.sentence: The film is a pastiche of classic horror movies.

pastoral.def: Relating to rural life or shepherds.
pastoral.sentence: The painting depicted a peaceful pastoral scene.

patent.sentence: It was a patent lie that everyone saw through.

pathetic.def: Arousing pity or sadness; miserably inadequate.
pathetic.sentence: His pathetic attempts at humor fell flat.

pathogenic.def: Causing or capable of causing disease.

pathological.def: Relating to or caused by disease; abnormal or compulsive.
pathological.sentence: His pathological lying made it impossible to trust anything he said.

pathology.def: The scientific study of diseases, or the structural and functional changes caused by disease.
pathology.sentence: The pathology report confirmed the presence of malignant cells.

pathos.def: A quality that evokes pity, sadness, or tenderness.
pathos.sentence: The film's pathos moved the audience to tears.

patois.def: A regional dialect or nonstandard language, especially one with low prestige.
patois.sentence: The villagers spoke a French patois that was hard for outsiders to understand.

patriarch.def: The male head of a family or tribe; an older man who is respected and authoritative.
patriarch.sentence: The patriarch of the family made all the important decisions.

patronize.def: To treat with condescension; to be a customer of a business.
patronize.sentence: She hated when her boss would patronize her with overly simple explanations.

patronizing.def: Treating someone with apparent kindness that betrays a feeling of superiority.
patronizing.sentence: His patronizing tone made her feel like a child.

paucity.sentence: The paucity of evidence led to the suspect's release.

pebble.def: A small, smooth, rounded stone, especially one worn by water.
pebble.sentence: She skipped a pebble across the surface of the lake.

peculiar.def: Strange or odd; unusual; belonging exclusively to one person or group.
peculiar.sentence: There was a peculiar smell coming from the basement.

pecuniary.def: Relating to or consisting of money.
pecuniary.sentence: The judge recused himself due to a pecuniary interest in the case.

pedagogical.sentence: The school adopted new pedagogical methods to improve student engagement.

pedagogue.def: A teacher, especially one who is strict or pedantic.
pedagogue.sentence: The old pedagogue insisted on memorizing dates and names.

pedagogy.def: The method and practice of teaching, especially as an academic subject.
pedagogy.sentence: She studied pedagogy to become a more effective educator.

pedant.def: A person who is excessively concerned with minor details and rules, especially in teaching.
pedant.sentence: The pedant corrected everyone's grammar, even in casual conversation.

pedantic.def: Excessively concerned with minor details or rules; overscrupulous.
pedantic.sentence: His pedantic remarks about the proper use of semicolons annoyed the class.

pedantry.def: Excessive concern with minor details and rules, especially in teaching.
pedantry.sentence: The professor's pedantry made his lectures tedious.

peddle.def: To sell goods, especially by traveling from place to place; to promote an idea persistently.
peddle.sentence: He peddled his homemade crafts at the local market.

pedestrian.def: Lacking inspiration or excitement; dull; ordinary.
pedestrian.sentence: The movie's plot was pedestrian and predictable.

peer.def: A person of the same age, status, or ability as another; a nobleman.
peer.sentence: Teenagers are often influenced by their peers.

peevish.def: Easily irritated, especially by unimportant things; irritable.
peevish.sentence: The peevish child complained about everything.

pejorative.sentence: The term 'nerd' can be used as a pejorative.

pellucid.sentence: Her pellucid explanation made the complex theory accessible.

penance.def: Voluntary self-punishment to express repentance for wrongdoing.
penance.sentence: As penance for his lies, he volunteered at the shelter.

penchant.def: A strong or habitual liking for something; a tendency.
penchant.sentence: She has a penchant for expensive shoes.

pendulous.sentence: The pendulous branches of the willow tree swayed gently in the breeze.

penitent.def: Feeling or showing sorrow and regret for having done wrong; repentant.
penitent.sentence: The penitent sinner confessed his misdeeds to the priest.

pensive.def: Engaged in deep or serious thought, often with a tinge of sadness.
pensive.sentence: She sat by the window, pensive and lost in memories.

penultimate.def: Second to last in a series or sequence.
penultimate.sentence: The penultimate chapter of the book revealed the killer's identity.

penumbra.def: A partial shadow or an area of partial illumination, as in an eclipse.
penumbra.sentence: During the solar eclipse, we stood in the penumbra and saw a crescent sun.

penurious.def: Extremely poor; poverty-stricken; also, miserly or stingy.
penurious.sentence: The penurious family could barely afford food and shelter.

penury.sentence: After losing his job, he fell into a state of penury.

peons.def: Unskilled laborers or workers of low status; also, people held in servitude.
peons.sentence: The plantation owner treated his workers like peons, paying them barely enough to survive.

per se.sentence: The law is not unjust per se, but its application can be problematic.

perceive.def: To become aware of something through the senses; to understand or interpret.
perceive.sentence: She perceived a subtle change in his tone of voice.

perch.def: To alight, rest, or sit on something, especially a high or narrow place.
perch.sentence: The bird perched on the fence, watching the garden.

percipient.def: Having the ability to perceive or understand things quickly and accurately; perceptive.
percipient.sentence: The percipient detective noticed the clue that others had missed.

peremptory.def: Leaving no opportunity for denial or refusal; imperative; also, dictatorial or dogmatic.
peremptory.sentence: The judge issued a peremptory order for the witness to testify.

perennial.def: Lasting for an indefinitely long time; enduring; also, (of plants) living for several years.
perennial.sentence: The perennial problem of poverty continues to plague the region.

perfidious.sentence: The perfidious ally betrayed the secret plans to the enemy.

perfidy.def: The quality of being deceitful and untrustworthy; treachery.
perfidy.sentence: His act of perfidy destroyed the trust that had taken years to build.

perfunctory.def: Carried out with a minimum of effort or reflection; cursory or mechanical.
perfunctory.sentence: He gave a perfunctory nod and continued reading his book.

perigee.def: The point in the orbit of the moon or a satellite at which it is nearest to the earth.
perigee.sentence: The moon appears larger and brighter when it is at perigee.

perilous.def: Full of danger or risk; hazardous.
perilous.sentence: The explorers embarked on a perilous journey through the jungle.

peripatetic.def: Traveling from place to place, especially working or based in various places.
peripatetic.sentence: The peripatetic teacher moved from school to school across the country.

peripheral.def: Relating to or situated on the edge or periphery; of secondary or minor importance.
peripheral.sentence: The issue was peripheral to the main debate and was quickly dismissed.

perjury.def: The offense of willfully telling an untruth in a court of law after having taken an oath.
perjury.sentence: The witness was charged with perjury for lying under oath.

permeable.def: Allowing liquids or gases to pass through.
permeable.sentence: The permeable membrane allowed water to flow through while blocking larger molecules.

permeate.def: To spread or flow throughout; to pervade.
permeate.sentence: The smell of fresh coffee permeated the entire house.

permeated.def: Past tense of permeate; spread or flowed throughout.
permeated.sentence: A sense of calm permeated the room after the meditation session.

permutation.def: A way, especially one of several possible variations, in which a set or number of things can be ordered or arranged.
permutation.sentence: The different permutations of the password made it hard to crack.

pernicious.def: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way.
pernicious.sentence: The pernicious influence of fake news can erode public trust.

peroration.def: The concluding part of a speech, typically intended to inspire enthusiasm.
peroration.sentence: The speaker's peroration moved the audience to tears.

perpetrate.def: To carry out or commit a harmful, illegal, or immoral action.
perpetrate.sentence: The thief perpetrated a series of burglaries in the neighborhood.

perpetuate.def: To cause something to continue indefinitely.
perpetuate.sentence: The myth was perpetuated by generations of storytellers.

perplex.def: To cause someone to feel completely baffled or confused.
perplex.sentence: The complex puzzle perplexed even the experts.

perquisite.def: A benefit or privilege enjoyed by someone as a result of their position.
perquisite.sentence: The executive's perquisites included a company car and a private jet.

perquisites.def: Plural of perquisite; benefits or privileges.
perquisites.sentence: The job offered many perquisites, such as free meals and gym membership.

perseverance.def: Persistence in doing something despite difficulty or delay in achieving success.
perseverance.sentence: Her perseverance in learning the piano paid off when she won the competition.

persevere.def: To continue in a course of action despite difficulty or opposition.
persevere.sentence: Despite the setbacks, he persevered and eventually completed the marathon.

personable.def: Having a pleasant appearance and manner; friendly and likable.
personable.sentence: The personable waiter made our dining experience enjoyable.

personified.def: To represent or embody a quality or concept in human form.
personified.sentence: She personified grace and elegance in her performance.

perspicacious.def: Having a ready insight into and understanding of things; shrewd.
perspicacious.sentence: The perspicacious detective quickly solved the mystery.

perspicacity.def: The quality of having a ready insight into things; shrewdness.
perspicacity.sentence: Her perspicacity in business dealings made her a successful entrepreneur.

pertinacious.def: Holding firmly to an opinion or a course of action; stubborn.
pertinacious.sentence: The pertinacious journalist refused to drop the story despite threats.

pertinent.def: Relevant or applicable to a particular matter.
pertinent.sentence: Please provide only pertinent information in your report.

perturb.def: To make someone anxious or unsettled; to disturb.
perturb.sentence: The sudden change in plans perturbed the entire team.

peruse.def: To read something carefully or thoroughly.
peruse.sentence: She perused the contract before signing it.

pervade.def: To spread through and be perceived in every part of something.
pervade.sentence: A sense of calm pervaded the room.

pervasive.def: Spreading widely throughout an area or a group of people.
pervasive.sentence: The pervasive influence of social media is undeniable.

perverse.def: Showing a deliberate and obstinate desire to behave in a way that is unreasonable or unacceptable.
perverse.sentence: He took perverse pleasure in proving others wrong.

pesky.sentence: Those pesky mosquitoes kept us awake all night.

pestilence.def: A fatal epidemic disease, especially bubonic plague.
pestilence.sentence: The pestilence swept through the city, leaving devastation in its wake.

petition.def: A formal written request, typically one signed by many people, appealing to authority.
petition.sentence: The residents submitted a petition to the city council for better street lighting.

petrous.def: Of or relating to the hard part of the temporal bone; also, resembling rock in hardness.
petrous.sentence: The petrous portion of the temporal bone protects the inner ear.

petulance.def: The quality of being childishly sulky or bad-tempered.
petulance.sentence: Her petulance was evident when she stomped her feet after being told no.

petulant.sentence: The petulant child refused to eat his vegetables.

phalanx.def: A group of people or things of a similar type forming a compact body or brought together for a common purpose.
phalanx.sentence: A phalanx of reporters awaited the celebrity's arrival.

phantasmagorical.def: Having a fantastic or deceptive appearance, as something in a dream or created by the imagination.
phantasmagorical.sentence: The phantasmagorical landscape seemed to shift and change before our eyes.

phenomena.def: Plural of phenomenon; observable facts or events.
phenomena.sentence: The scientists studied various natural phenomena.

phenomenon.def: A fact or event that is observed to exist or happen, especially one whose cause is in question.
phenomenon.sentence: The aurora borealis is a beautiful natural phenomenon.

Philadelphia lawyer.def: A lawyer who is particularly clever and skilled in legal maneuvering.
Philadelphia lawyer.sentence: Only a Philadelphia lawyer could find a loophole in that contract.

philander.def: (of a man) to engage in casual sexual relationships with many women.
philander.sentence: He was known to philander despite being married.

philanthropist.def: A person who seeks to promote the welfare of others, especially by donating money to good causes.
philanthropist.sentence: The philanthropist donated millions to build a new hospital.

philanthropy.def: The desire to promote the welfare of others, expressed especially by the generous donation of money to good causes.
philanthropy.sentence: Her philanthropy extended to supporting education and the arts.

philistine.def: A person who is hostile or indifferent to culture and the arts.
philistine.sentence: He was dismissed as a philistine for his lack of appreciation for classical music.

phlegmatic.def: Having an unemotional and stolidly calm disposition.
phlegmatic.sentence: The phlegmatic captain remained calm during the storm.

phobia.def: An extreme or irrational fear of or aversion to something.
phobia.sentence: Her phobia of spiders made it impossible to enter the basement.

phoenix.def: A mythical bird that rises from its ashes, symbolizing rebirth or renewal.
phoenix.sentence: After the fire, the city rebuilt like a phoenix from the ashes.

phony.sentence: His phony smile didn't fool anyone.

physiognomy.def: A person's facial features or expression, especially when regarded as indicative of character or ethnic origin.
physiognomy.sentence: The detective studied the suspect's physiognomy for clues.

physiological.def: Relating to the branch of biology that deals with the normal functions of living organisms and their parts.
physiological.sentence: The physiological effects of exercise include increased heart rate.

picaresque.def: Relating to an episodic style of fiction dealing with the adventures of a rough and dishonest but appealing hero.
picaresque.sentence: The novel is a picaresque tale of a rogue's travels.

picayune.def: Of little value or significance; petty.

picturesque.def: Visually attractive, especially in a quaint or charming way.
picturesque.sentence: The village was picturesque with its cobblestone streets.

piebald.def: Having irregular patches of two colors, typically black and white.
piebald.sentence: The piebald horse stood out in the field.

pied.def: Having two or more different colors.
pied.sentence: The pied bird had a mix of black and white feathers.

piety.def: The quality of being religious or reverent.
piety.sentence: Her piety was evident in her daily prayers.

pilfer.def: To steal (something) of little value or in small quantities.
pilfer.sentence: He would pilfer snacks from the pantry.

pillage.def: To rob a place using violence, especially in wartime.

pillory.def: to publicly criticize or ridicule someone harshly.
pillory.sentence: The journalist was pilloried for her controversial article.

pine.def: to yearn intensely or languish from longing.
pine.sentence: She pined for her homeland after moving abroad.

pinnacle.def: the highest point of development or achievement.
pinnacle.sentence: Winning the Nobel Prize was the pinnacle of her career.

pioneering.def: involving new ideas or methods that are ahead of their time.
pioneering.sentence: The pioneering research led to a breakthrough in cancer treatment.

pious.def: devoutly religious or showing strong religious devotion.
pious.sentence: The pious monk spent hours in prayer each day.

piquant.def: having a pleasantly sharp or spicy taste; stimulating interest.
piquant.sentence: The piquant sauce added a kick to the dish.

pique.def: a feeling of irritation or resentment resulting from a slight.
pique.sentence: He left the party in a fit of pique after being ignored.

pitch.def: the degree of highness or lowness of a sound; also a persuasive talk.
pitch.sentence: The salesman gave a compelling pitch for the new product.

pitfall.def: a hidden or unsuspected danger or difficulty.
pitfall.sentence: One common pitfall of investing is emotional decision-making.

pith.def: the essential or central part of something.
pith.sentence: The pith of his argument was that education is key.

pithy.sentence: Her pithy remarks often left the audience thinking.

pittance.def: a very small or inadequate amount of money.
pittance.sentence: He worked long hours for a mere pittance.

pivotal.def: of crucial importance in relation to the development or success of something.
pivotal.sentence: The pivotal moment in the game was the last-minute goal.

placard.def: a sign or notice for public display.

placate.def: to make someone less angry or hostile; to appease.
placate.sentence: The manager tried to placate the angry customer with a refund.

placebo.def: a harmless substance given as a dummy treatment in medical trials.
placebo.sentence: The placebo effect can sometimes produce real improvements.

placid.def: calm and peaceful, with little movement or activity.
placid.sentence: The placid lake reflected the surrounding mountains.

plaintiff.def: a person who brings a case against another in a court of law.
plaintiff.sentence: The plaintiff claimed damages for breach of contract.

plaintive.sentence: The plaintive cry of the lost child touched everyone's heart.

plangent.def: loud, resonant, and often mournful in sound.
plangent.sentence: The plangent bells tolled for the fallen soldiers.

plastic.def: capable of being molded or shaped; also synthetic material.
plastic.sentence: The clay was still plastic and easy to work with.

plasticity.def: the quality of being easily shaped or molded.
plasticity.sentence: The plasticity of the brain allows it to adapt to new experiences.

platitude.def: a trite, meaningless, or prosaic statement.
platitude.sentence: His speech was full of platitudes like 'follow your dreams'.

platonic.def: of or relating to a close relationship that is not sexual.
platonic.sentence: They had a platonic friendship despite the rumors.

plaudits.sentence: The performance received plaudits from the critics.

plausible.sentence: Her explanation for being late was plausible, so I didn't question it further.

plea.def: a request made in an urgent and emotional manner.
plea.sentence: The mother made a heartfelt plea for the safe return of her missing child.

plea bargain.def: an agreement between a prosecutor and a defendant whereby the defendant pleads guilty to a lesser charge in exchange for a more lenient sentence.
plea bargain.sentence: The defendant accepted a plea bargain to avoid a lengthy trial.

plead.def: to make an emotional appeal or request earnestly.
plead.sentence: He pleaded with his parents to let him go to the concert.

plebeian.def: of or belonging to the common people; unrefined or coarse.
plebeian.sentence: The aristocrat looked down on plebeian tastes in entertainment.

plebian or plebeian.def: variant spelling of plebeian; of or belonging to the common people.
plebian or plebeian.sentence: The critic dismissed the film as plebian and lacking artistic merit.

plenipotentiary.def: a person, especially a diplomat, invested with full power to act on behalf of their government.
plenipotentiary.sentence: The ambassador was appointed plenipotentiary to negotiate the treaty.

plethora.def: a large or excessive amount of something.
plethora.sentence: The buffet offered a plethora of dishes, making it hard to choose.

pliant.def: bending easily; flexible or easily influenced.
pliant.sentence: The pliant willow branches swayed in the wind.

plight.def: a dangerous, difficult, or otherwise unfortunate situation.
plight.sentence: The documentary highlighted the plight of endangered species.

plod.def: to walk or move with heavy, slow steps, especially as a result of exhaustion or difficulty.
plod.sentence: After the long hike, we plodded back to the campsite.

plodding.def: slow-moving and unexciting; laborious and monotonous.
plodding.sentence: The plodding pace of the lecture made it hard to stay awake.

ploy.def: a cunning plan or action designed to turn a situation to one's own advantage.
ploy.sentence: His offer to help was just a ploy to get inside information.

pluck.sentence: The young hero showed great pluck in standing up to the bully.

plucky.def: having or showing determined courage in the face of difficulties.
plucky.sentence: The plucky little dog chased away the much larger intruder.

plumb.def: to measure the depth of something, especially water; to explore or examine thoroughly.
plumb.sentence: The scientist plumbed the depths of the ocean to study marine life.

plummet.def: to fall or drop straight down at high speed.
plummet.sentence: Stock prices plummeted after the company announced its losses.

plutocracy.def: government by the wealthy; a society ruled by the rich.
plutocracy.sentence: Critics argue that the country has become a plutocracy where money dictates policy.

poignant.def: evoking a keen sense of sadness or regret; emotionally moving.
poignant.sentence: The film's poignant ending left the audience in tears.

poise.def: graceful and elegant bearing in a person; composure and dignity.
poise.sentence: She handled the difficult situation with remarkable poise.

polarize.def: to cause to divide into two sharply contrasting groups or sets of opinions.
polarize.sentence: The controversial issue polarized the community into opposing factions.

polarized.def: divided into two sharply contrasting groups or sets of opinions.
polarized.sentence: The polarized debate showed no signs of reaching a consensus.

polemic.def: a strong verbal or written attack on someone or something.
polemic.sentence: His book is a polemic against the current education system.

polemical.def: of or involving strongly critical or disputatious writing or speech.
polemical.sentence: The article was highly polemical, attacking the government's policies.

politesse.def: formal politeness and courtesy; good breeding.
politesse.sentence: The diplomat's politesse was appreciated during the tense negotiations.

politic.def: Sagacious in promoting a policy or plan; shrewd and diplomatic.
politic.sentence: It was politic of her to avoid discussing the controversial topic at the dinner party.

polyglot.sentence: The polyglot ambassador could converse fluently in six languages.

polymath.def: A person of wide-ranging knowledge or learning.
polymath.sentence: Leonardo da Vinci is the quintessential polymath, excelling in art, science, and engineering.

pomp.sentence: The royal wedding was full of pomp and pageantry.

pomposity.def: The quality of being pompous; self-importance.
pomposity.sentence: His pomposity alienated his colleagues, who found him insufferable.

pompous.def: Affectedly and irritatingly grand, solemn, or self-important.
pompous.sentence: The professor's pompous lecture was more about his own brilliance than the subject.

ponder.def: To think about something carefully, especially before making a decision.
ponder.sentence: She pondered the offer for days before accepting.

ponderous.def: Slow and clumsy because of great weight; dull and laborious.
ponderous.sentence: The ponderous elephant moved slowly through the mud.

pontificate.def: To express one's opinions in a way considered annoyingly pompous and dogmatic.
pontificate.sentence: He would pontificate about politics at every family gathering.

populist.def: Relating to or characteristic of a political approach that appeals to ordinary people.
populist.sentence: The populist candidate promised to fight for the working class.

porous.def: Having tiny holes that allow liquid or air to pass through.
porous.sentence: The porous soil absorbed the rainwater quickly.

portend.def: To be a sign or warning that something, especially something momentous or calamitous, is likely to happen.
portend.sentence: The dark clouds portended a severe storm.

portentous.def: Of or like a portent; ominously significant or momentous.
portentous.sentence: The portentous silence before the announcement made everyone nervous.

poseur.def: A person who pretends to be what they are not; an affected person.
poseur.sentence: He was dismissed as a poseur who knew nothing about real art.

posit.def: To assume as a fact; to put forward as a basis of argument.
posit.sentence: The theory posits that life originated in the oceans.

posthumous.def: Occurring, awarded, or appearing after the death of the person concerned.
posthumous.sentence: He received a posthumous medal for bravery.

posture.def: A particular position of the body; a way of behaving that is intended to impress others.
posture.sentence: Good posture is important for back health.

potent.def: Having great power, influence, or effect.
potent.sentence: The medicine is potent and should be taken only as directed.

potentate.def: A monarch or ruler, especially an autocratic one.
potentate.sentence: The potentate ruled his kingdom with an iron fist.

potential.def: Having the capacity to develop into something in the future; latent.
potential.sentence: The young athlete has great potential.

potpourri.def: A mixture of dried petals and spices used to scent a room; a miscellaneous collection.
potpourri.sentence: The book is a potpourri of essays on various topics.

powwow.def: A conference or meeting for discussion, especially among Native Americans.
powwow.sentence: The leaders held a powwow to discuss the treaty.

prÃ©cis.def: A summary or abstract of a text or speech.
prÃ©cis.sentence: She wrote a précis of the article for her class.

practitioner.def: A person actively engaged in an art, discipline, or profession.
practitioner.sentence: She is a practitioner of traditional Chinese medicine.

pragmatic.def: Dealing with things sensibly and realistically; practical.
pragmatic.sentence: We need a pragmatic approach to solve this problem.

prate.def: to talk excessively and pointlessly; to chatter.
prate.sentence: The old man would prate on for hours about his youth, boring everyone in the room.

prattle.def: to talk in a foolish or simple-minded way; to babble.
prattle.sentence: The toddler prattled happily about her imaginary friend.

pre-empt.def: to take action in order to prevent an anticipated event from happening; to forestall.
pre-empt.sentence: The company pre-empted the strike by offering a generous pay raise.

preamble.def: an introductory statement or preliminary explanation.
preamble.sentence: The preamble to the Constitution outlines the fundamental purposes of the document.

precarious.def: not securely held or in position; dangerously likely to fall or collapse.
precarious.sentence: The climber found himself in a precarious position on the narrow ledge.

preceded.def: came before in time or order.
preceded.sentence: The calm that preceded the storm was eerie and unsettling.

precedence.def: the condition of being considered more important than someone or something else; priority.
precedence.sentence: In emergencies, saving lives takes precedence over property protection.

precedent.def: an earlier event or action that is regarded as an example or guide to be considered in subsequent similar circumstances.
precedent.sentence: The court ruling set a precedent for future cases involving digital privacy.

precept.def: a general rule intended to regulate behavior or thought.
precept.sentence: The golden rule is a precept found in many cultures.

precious.def: of great value; not to be wasted or treated carelessly.
precious.sentence: Time is a precious resource that should be used wisely.

precipice.def: a very steep rock face or cliff, especially a tall one.
precipice.sentence: The hiker stood at the edge of the precipice, gazing into the vast canyon below.

precipitate.def: to cause (an event or situation) to happen suddenly, unexpectedly, or prematurely.
precipitate.sentence: The economic crisis was precipitated by the collapse of the housing market.

precipitous.def: dangerously high or steep; sudden and dramatic.
precipitous.sentence: The stock market experienced a precipitous decline after the announcement.

precis.def: a summary or abstract of a text or speech.
precis.sentence: The student wrote a precis of the article for her assignment.

précis.def: a summary or abstract of a text or speech.
précis.sentence: The student wrote a précis of the article for her assignment.

preclude.def: to prevent from happening; to make impossible.
preclude.sentence: The heavy snow precluded any possibility of travel.

precocious.def: having developed certain abilities or proclivities at an earlier age than usual.
precocious.sentence: The precocious child could read at the age of three.

precursor.def: a person or thing that comes before another of the same kind; a forerunner.
precursor.sentence: The steam engine was a precursor to modern locomotives.

predicament.def: a difficult, unpleasant, or embarrassing situation.
predicament.sentence: He found himself in a predicament when he lost his wallet in a foreign country.

predilection.def: a preference or special liking for something; a bias in favor of something.
predilection.sentence: She has a predilection for classical music over modern genres.

predisposed.def: tending to be affected by something; having a tendency or inclination.
predisposed.sentence: People with a family history of heart disease are predisposed to the condition.

predisposition.def: a tendency to suffer from a particular condition, hold a particular attitude, or act in a particular way.
predisposition.sentence: There is a genetic predisposition to certain types of cancer.

preempt.def: to take action in order to prevent an anticipated event from happening; to forestall.
preempt.sentence: The government preempted the crisis by implementing new regulations.

preempt or pre-empt.def: to take action in order to prevent an anticipated event from happening; to forestall.
preempt or pre-empt.sentence: The government preempted the crisis by implementing new regulations.

preemptive.def: serving or intended to preempt or forestall something, especially to prevent attack by disabling the enemy.
preemptive.sentence: The country launched a preemptive strike against the rebel forces.

preen.def: to groom or trim feathers with the beak, or to make oneself neat and tidy; also, to feel self-satisfaction.
preen.sentence: The bird spent hours preening its feathers after the rain.

prehensile.def: capable of grasping or holding, especially by wrapping around an object.
prehensile.sentence: The monkey used its prehensile tail to swing from branch to branch.

prelude.def: an introductory event, action, or piece of music that precedes something more important.
prelude.sentence: The opening act was a prelude to the main concert.

premise.def: a proposition or statement that serves as the basis for an argument or conclusion.
premise.sentence: The detective's premise was that the butler had a motive.

premonition.def: a strong feeling that something, especially something unpleasant, is about to happen.
premonition.sentence: She had a premonition that the flight would be delayed.

prerogative.def: a special right or privilege exclusive to a particular person or group.
prerogative.sentence: It is the manager's prerogative to make the final decision.

presage.def: to be a sign or warning of something that will happen in the future.
presage.sentence: The dark clouds presaged a coming storm.

prescience.def: the ability to know or predict what will happen in the future.
prescience.sentence: The prophet's prescience was admired by many.

prescient.def: having knowledge of events before they take place; foresighted.
prescient.sentence: Her prescient warnings about the market crash saved many investors.

presentiment.def: a vague sense of something about to happen, often something negative.
presentiment.sentence: He had a presentiment that the meeting would not go well.

prestigious.def: having a high reputation; esteemed and respected.
prestigious.sentence: She was awarded a prestigious scholarship to study abroad.

presume.def: to assume something is true without proof, or to take liberties.
presume.sentence: I presume you have finished your homework.

presumption.def: an assumption that something is true, or behavior perceived as arrogant.
presumption.sentence: The presumption of innocence is a cornerstone of the legal system.

presumptive.def: based on presumption or probability; likely to be true.
presumptive.sentence: The presumptive heir to the throne was well-prepared for his role.

presumptuous.def: overstepping bounds; showing excessive boldness or arrogance.
presumptuous.sentence: It was presumptuous of him to assume he would get the job.

pretense.def: a false show or claim intended to deceive or hide the truth.
pretense.sentence: Under the pretense of friendship, he gathered information.

pretentious.def: attempting to impress by affecting greater importance or merit than is actually possessed.
pretentious.sentence: His pretentious remarks about art annoyed everyone.

preternatural.def: beyond what is normal or natural; extraordinary.
preternatural.sentence: The cat seemed to have a preternatural ability to sense danger.

pretext.def: a false reason used to justify an action or to conceal the real reason.
pretext.sentence: He used a headache as a pretext to leave the party early.

prevail.def: to prove more powerful than opposing forces; to be widespread.
prevail.sentence: Justice will prevail in the end.

prevalent.def: widespread in a particular area or at a particular time.
prevalent.sentence: The flu is prevalent during the winter months.

prevaricate.def: to speak or act in an evasive way; to lie or avoid the truth.
prevaricate.sentence: The politician prevaricated when asked about the scandal.

prevarication.def: the act of avoiding the truth; a lie or evasion.
prevarication.sentence: His prevarication only made the situation worse.

primacy.def: the state of being first in importance, rank, or order.
primacy.sentence: The primacy of safety cannot be overstated.

prime.def: of the best possible quality; first in rank or importance.
prime.sentence: The prime location of the hotel made it very popular.

primed.def: prepared and ready for action or use.
primed.sentence: The team was primed for the final match after weeks of intense training.

primordial.def: existing at or from the beginning of time; primeval.
primordial.sentence: The primordial soup is thought to have given rise to the first life forms.

principled.def: acting in accordance with morality and rules of conduct.
principled.sentence: She is a principled leader who never compromises her values.

pristine.sentence: The beach remained pristine, untouched by tourism.

privation.def: a state of extreme poverty or lack of necessities.
privation.sentence: The family endured years of privation during the war.

prize.sentence: She prizes her collection of rare books above all else.

probity.def: the quality of having strong moral principles; honesty and decency.
probity.sentence: The judge was known for her probity and fairness.

problematic.def: constituting a problem; difficult to solve or decide.
problematic.sentence: The lack of funding made the project problematic.

proclamation.def: a public or official announcement dealing with a matter of great importance.
proclamation.sentence: The king issued a proclamation declaring a national holiday.

proclivity.def: a tendency to choose or do something regularly; an inclination.
proclivity.sentence: He has a proclivity for exaggeration.

procrastinate.def: to delay or postpone action; put off doing something.
procrastinate.sentence: She tends to procrastinate when faced with difficult tasks.

prodigal.def: spending money or resources freely and recklessly; wastefully extravagant.
prodigal.sentence: The prodigal son squandered his inheritance.

prodigality.def: the quality of being extravagant or wasteful.
prodigality.sentence: His prodigality left him bankrupt within a year.

prodigious.def: remarkably or impressively great in extent, size, or degree.
prodigious.sentence: She had a prodigious talent for music.

prodigy.def: a person, especially a young one, with exceptional qualities or abilities.
prodigy.sentence: Mozart was a child prodigy, composing music at age five.

profane.def: showing contempt for sacred things; irreverent.
profane.sentence: His profane language shocked the audience.

proffer.def: to offer or present for acceptance.
proffer.sentence: He proffered his hand in friendship.

proficient.def: competent or skilled in doing or using something.
proficient.sentence: She is proficient in three languages.

profligacy.sentence: The government's profligacy led to a huge national debt.

profligate.def: recklessly extravagant or wasteful in the use of resources.
profligate.sentence: The profligate heir spent his fortune on lavish parties.

profound.def: very great or intense; having deep meaning.
profound.sentence: The book had a profound impact on my thinking.

profundity.def: deep insight or great depth of knowledge or thought.
profundity.sentence: The philosopher's profundity amazed his students.

profuse.def: produced or given in large amounts; abundant.
profuse.sentence: She offered profuse apologies for the mistake.

profusion.def: an abundance or large quantity of something.
profusion.sentence: The garden had a profusion of flowers in bloom.

progenitor.def: a person or thing from which others are descended; an ancestor.
progenitor.sentence: The chimpanzee is a progenitor of modern humans.

progenitors.def: Ancestors or predecessors in a family or lineage.
progenitors.sentence: The progenitors of the modern computer were the early calculating machines.

progeny.sentence: The farmer was proud of his prize bull's progeny.

prognosticate.def: To predict or forecast something, especially based on present indications.
prognosticate.sentence: Economists prognosticate a recession next year.

prognostication.sentence: His prognostication of a market crash proved accurate.

prohibition.def: The act of forbidding something, or a law that forbids it.
prohibition.sentence: The prohibition of smoking in public places improved air quality.

prohibitive.def: So high or expensive as to prevent purchase or use.
prohibitive.sentence: The cost of the new software was prohibitive for small businesses.

proletarian.def: Relating to the working class, especially those who do manual labor.
proletarian.sentence: The proletarian movement fought for better working conditions.

proliferate.def: To increase rapidly in number; multiply.
proliferate.sentence: Social media platforms have proliferated in the last decade.

prolific.def: Producing many works, results, or offspring.
prolific.sentence: Shakespeare was a prolific playwright, writing dozens of plays.

prolix.sentence: His prolix speech bored the audience.

prologue.def: An introductory section of a literary or dramatic work.
prologue.sentence: The prologue set the stage for the epic tale.

prolong.def: To extend the duration of something.
prolong.sentence: The medication helped prolong her life.

prominent.sentence: She played a prominent role in the negotiations.

prompt.sentence: The prompt response from the fire department saved the building.

promulgate.def: To make widely known; to proclaim.
promulgate.sentence: The government promulgated a new set of regulations.

prone.def: Likely to suffer from or do something; having a tendency.
prone.sentence: He is prone to headaches in bright light.

pronounced.sentence: There was a pronounced difference in their opinions.

propagate.def: To spread and promote an idea, theory, etc., widely.
propagate.sentence: The group propagated false information about the vaccine.

propensity.def: A natural tendency to behave in a particular way.
propensity.sentence: She has a propensity for exaggeration.

prophetic.def: Accurately predicting what will happen in the future.
prophetic.sentence: His prophetic words came true years later.

propinquity.def: Nearness in place or time; proximity.
propinquity.sentence: The propinquity of the two houses made them good neighbors.

propitiate.def: To win or regain the favor of someone, especially a god or spirit, by doing something pleasing.
propitiate.sentence: They offered sacrifices to propitiate the angry gods.

propitious.def: Indicating a good chance of success; favorable.
propitious.sentence: The clear skies were a propitious sign for the launch.

proponent.def: A person who advocates for something.
proponent.sentence: She is a strong proponent of renewable energy.

proposition.def: A statement or idea that is put forward for consideration.
proposition.sentence: His proposition to reduce taxes was debated in parliament.

propriety.def: Conformity to socially accepted standards of behavior or morals.
propriety.sentence: She always acted with propriety, even in the most informal settings.

prosaic.def: Lacking poetic beauty; commonplace or dull.
prosaic.sentence: The view from the window was prosaic, just a row of identical houses.

proscribe.def: To forbid or condemn, especially by official decree.
proscribe.sentence: The new law proscribes the use of cell phones while driving.

proselytize.def: To convert or attempt to convert someone from one religion, belief, or opinion to another.
proselytize.sentence: The missionaries went door-to-door to proselytize their faith.

prospective.def: Likely to happen or become something in the future.
prospective.sentence: The company interviewed several prospective candidates for the job.

protean.def: Able to change form or adapt to different roles; versatile.
protean.sentence: The protean actor could play both comedic and tragic roles with ease.

protocol.def: A system of rules governing formal occasions or official procedures.
protocol.sentence: Diplomatic protocol requires that ambassadors present their credentials to the head of state.

protracted.def: Lasting longer than expected or usual; prolonged.
protracted.sentence: The protracted negotiations finally ended after six months.

provident.def: Showing foresight and careful planning for the future.
provident.sentence: A provident person saves money for emergencies.

providential.def: Occurring at a favorable time; opportune or lucky, as if by divine intervention.
providential.sentence: Their providential escape from the burning building seemed like a miracle.

provincial.def: Of or concerning a province; limited in outlook, narrow-minded.
provincial.sentence: His provincial attitudes clashed with the cosmopolitan city life.

provisional.def: Arranged or existing for the present, possibly to be changed later; temporary.
provisional.sentence: The provisional government was established until elections could be held.

provocation.def: Action or speech that makes someone annoyed or angry, especially deliberately.
provocation.sentence: He responded calmly to the provocation, refusing to fight.

provocative.def: Causing annoyance, anger, or strong reaction; intentionally stimulating.
provocative.sentence: The artist's provocative painting sparked a heated debate.

prowess.def: Skill or expertise in a particular activity or field.
prowess.sentence: Her athletic prowess earned her a spot on the national team.

proximity.def: Nearness in space, time, or relationship.
proximity.sentence: The proximity of the airport made the neighborhood noisy.

proxy.def: The authority to represent someone else, especially in voting.
proxy.sentence: She voted by proxy because she was out of the country.

prudent.def: Acting with or showing care and thought for the future.
prudent.sentence: It is prudent to wear a seatbelt while driving.

prune.def: To trim (a tree, shrub, or bush) by cutting away dead or overgrown branches.
prune.sentence: The gardener pruned the rose bushes to encourage new growth.

prurient.def: Having or encouraging an excessive interest in sexual matters.
prurient.sentence: The magazine was criticized for its prurient content.

pucker.def: To contract into wrinkles or folds.
pucker.sentence: She puckered her lips to apply lipstick.

puerile.sentence: His puerile jokes annoyed the adults at the party.

pugilism.sentence: Pugilism requires discipline, strength, and strategy.

pugilist.def: A boxer, especially a professional one.
pugilist.sentence: The pugilist trained daily for the championship match.

pugnacious.def: Eager or quick to argue, quarrel, or fight.
pugnacious.sentence: The pugnacious dog barked at every passerby.

puissance.sentence: The nation's economic puissance was evident in its global trade dominance.

puissant.def: Having great power or influence; mighty.
puissant.sentence: The puissant ruler commanded the loyalty of millions.

pulchritude.def: Physical beauty, especially of a woman.
pulchritude.sentence: Her pulchritude was admired by all who saw her.

punctilious.def: Showing great attention to detail or correct behavior.
punctilious.sentence: The punctilious host ensured every guest's glass was filled.

pundit.def: An expert or commentator who gives opinions in the media.
pundit.sentence: The political pundit predicted a landslide victory.

pungent.def: Having a sharply strong taste or smell.
pungent.sentence: The pungent aroma of garlic filled the kitchen.

punitive.sentence: The court imposed punitive damages on the company.

puny.sentence: The puny kitten could barely lift its head.

purist.def: A person who insists on absolute adherence to traditional rules or structures.
purist.sentence: The language purist refused to accept new slang.

purport.def: To claim or appear to be something, often falsely.
purport.sentence: The document purports to be an official decree.

pursuant.def: Following or in accordance with something.
pursuant.sentence: Pursuant to the agreement, payments were made monthly.

pusillanimous.def: Showing a lack of courage or determination; timid.
pusillanimous.sentence: The pusillanimous soldier fled the battlefield.

put the cart before the horse.def: To do things in the wrong order.
put the cart before the horse.sentence: By buying furniture before finding an apartment, he put the cart before the horse.

putrefy.def: To decay or rot, producing a foul smell.
putrefy.sentence: The abandoned food began to putrefy in the sun.

putrid.def: Decaying or rotting and having a foul smell.

pyrrhic.def: Relating to a victory won at too great a cost.
pyrrhic.sentence: The battle was a pyrrhic success, with heavy losses.

Pyrrhic victory.def: A victory that inflicts such a devastating toll on the victor that it is equivalent to defeat.
Pyrrhic victory.sentence: Winning the lawsuit was a Pyrrhic victory, as legal fees bankrupted the company.

quack.def: A person who falsely claims to have medical skill.
quack.sentence: The quack sold fake remedies to desperate patients.

quaff.def: To drink a beverage heartily or in large gulps.
quaff.sentence: He quaffed the ale in one long gulp.

quagmire.def: A soft boggy area of land; a difficult or precarious situation.
quagmire.sentence: The project became a quagmire of delays and disputes.

quail.def: To feel or show fear or apprehension.
quail.sentence: She quailed at the thought of speaking in public.

qualification.def: A condition or attribute that modifies or restricts something; a skill or credential.
qualification.sentence: His only qualification for the job was his enthusiasm.

qualified.def: Having the necessary skills or credentials; limited or restricted.
qualified.sentence: She is a qualified nurse with ten years of experience.

qualify.def: To meet the necessary requirements; to limit or modify a statement.
qualify.sentence: He qualified for the marathon by finishing a half-marathon.

qualm.def: A feeling of doubt, unease, or misgiving about one's actions.
qualm.sentence: He had no qualms about lying to protect his friend.

quandary.def: a state of perplexity or uncertainty.
quandary.sentence: She was in a quandary about which job offer to accept.

quarantine.def: a state of isolation to prevent the spread of disease.
quarantine.sentence: The ship was placed under quarantine for two weeks.

quarry.def: a place where stone is extracted from the ground.
quarry.sentence: The workers blasted rock at the quarry.

quash.sentence: The judge quashed the indictment due to lack of evidence.

quay.def: a wharf or platform for loading and unloading ships.
quay.sentence: The cargo was unloaded onto the quay.

quell.def: to put an end to, typically by force.
quell.sentence: The police quelled the riot with tear gas.

quenching.def: the act of extinguishing or satisfying a thirst.
quenching.sentence: The quenching of his thirst with cold water was refreshing.

querulous.sentence: The querulous child whined about everything.

query.def: a question, especially one expressing doubt.
query.sentence: She raised a query about the accuracy of the report.

queue.def: a line of people or vehicles waiting for something.
queue.sentence: We stood in a long queue for concert tickets.

quibble.sentence: They quibbled over the price of the used car.

quiescence.def: a state of inactivity or dormancy.
quiescence.sentence: The volcano's quiescence lasted for centuries.

quiescent.def: in a state of inactivity or dormancy.
quiescent.sentence: The quiescent patient lay still in bed.

quintessential.def: representing the most perfect example of a quality.
quintessential.sentence: Paris is the quintessential romantic city.

quip.sentence: She made a clever quip that made everyone laugh.

quirky.sentence: His quirky sense of humor set him apart.

quisling.def: a traitor who collaborates with an enemy force.
quisling.sentence: The quisling was executed after the war.

quiver.def: to tremble or shake with a slight rapid motion.

quixotic.sentence: His quixotic quest for perfection doomed the project.

quizzical.sentence: He gave her a quizzical look when she mentioned the unicorn.

quorum.def: the minimum number of members needed to conduct business.
quorum.sentence: The meeting was adjourned for lack of a quorum.

quotidian.def: of or occurring every day; commonplace.
quotidian.sentence: She found beauty in the quotidian routines of life.

rabid.sentence: The rabid fans stormed the field after the victory.

raconteur.def: a person who tells anecdotes in a skillful and amusing way.
raconteur.sentence: The raconteur held the audience spellbound with his stories.

radical.def: relating to or affecting the fundamental nature of something; far-reaching.
radical.sentence: The company underwent a radical restructuring.

raffish.def: Having a disreputable or unconventional charm.
raffish.sentence: His raffish grin and rumpled suit made him the life of the party.

raft.sentence: She received a raft of complaints after the policy change.

ragamuffin.def: A person, typically a child, in ragged, dirty clothes.
ragamuffin.sentence: The little ragamuffin played happily in the alley despite his tattered coat.

rail.def: To complain or protest strongly and bitterly.
rail.sentence: He railed against the injustice of the decision.

raiment.sentence: The king's raiment was embroidered with gold thread.

rakish.def: Having a dashing, slightly disreputable appearance or manner.
rakish.sentence: He wore his hat at a rakish angle, looking every bit the adventurer.

ramification.def: A complex or unwelcome consequence of an action or event.
ramification.sentence: The ramification of the merger was a massive layoff.

ramifications.def: Plural of ramification; complex or unwelcome consequences.
ramifications.sentence: The policy had far-reaching ramifications for the economy.

rampant.sentence: Corruption was rampant in the regime.

rampart.def: A defensive wall or protective barrier.
rampart.sentence: The castle's ramparts offered a view of the surrounding countryside.

rancorous.sentence: The rancorous debate ended with both sides refusing to speak.

ranger.def: A keeper of a park, forest, or area of countryside.
ranger.sentence: The ranger led the hikers through the national park.

rankle.def: To cause persistent annoyance or resentment.
rankle.sentence: His comment continued to rankle her long after the conversation.

rankled.def: Past tense of rankle; caused persistent annoyance or resentment.
rankled.sentence: The unfair criticism rankled him for days.

ranks.def: The members of a group or organization.
ranks.sentence: She rose through the ranks to become CEO.

rapacious.sentence: The rapacious corporation exploited the natural resources without regard.

rapprochement.def: An establishment or resumption of harmonious relations.
rapprochement.sentence: The two countries sought a rapprochement after years of conflict.

rapt.sentence: The audience was rapt during the pianist's performance.

rapturous.def: Characterized by intense pleasure or joy.
rapturous.sentence: The crowd gave a rapturous applause at the end of the show.

rarefied.def: Belonging to a select, refined, or exclusive group.
rarefied.sentence: He moved in rarefied circles of high society.

rash.def: Acting or done without careful consideration.
rash.sentence: It was a rash decision to quit his job without a backup plan.

rational.def: Based on reason rather than emotion.
rational.sentence: She made a rational choice to invest in the stable fund.

rationale.def: A set of reasons or a logical basis for a course of action.
rationale.sentence: The rationale behind the new policy was to reduce costs.

rationalize.def: To attempt to explain or justify (behavior or an attitude) with logical reasons, even if these are not appropriate.
rationalize.sentence: He tried to rationalize his procrastination by claiming he worked better under pressure.

raucous.def: Making or constituting a disturbingly harsh and loud noise.
raucous.sentence: The raucous laughter from the bar could be heard down the street.

ravage.def: to cause severe and extensive damage to
ravage.sentence: The hurricane ravaged the coastal town, leaving destruction in its wake.

ravenous.sentence: After the long hike, the campers were ravenous and devoured their meals.

raze.sentence: The old factory was razed to make way for a new housing development.

reactant.def: a substance that takes part in and undergoes change during a chemical reaction
reactant.sentence: In the lab, we mixed the reactants and observed the resulting reaction.

readily.sentence: She readily agreed to help with the project.

realization.def: an act of becoming fully aware of something as a fact
realization.sentence: It came as a sudden realization that he had been wrong all along.

realm.def: a field or domain of activity or interest
realm.sentence: The discovery opened up new realms of scientific inquiry.

reap.def: to receive as a result of one's own actions
reap.sentence: If you work hard, you will reap the benefits.

rebuke.def: to express sharp disapproval or criticism of someone because of their behavior or actions
rebuke.sentence: The teacher rebuked the student for talking during the exam.

rebus.def: a puzzle in which words are represented by pictures or symbols
rebus.sentence: The children enjoyed solving the rebus in the newspaper.

rebut.def: to claim or prove that something is false
rebut.sentence: The lawyer rebutted the witness's testimony with new evidence.

rebuttal.def: a statement that refutes or contradicts a claim
rebuttal.sentence: Her rebuttal was so convincing that the jury changed their minds.

recalcitrant.def: having an obstinately uncooperative attitude toward authority or discipline
recalcitrant.sentence: The recalcitrant student refused to follow the classroom rules.

recant.def: to withdraw or renounce a statement or belief formally
recant.sentence: Under pressure, the witness recanted his earlier testimony.

recapitulate.def: to summarize and state again the main points of something
recapitulate.sentence: At the end of the lecture, the professor recapitulated the key concepts.

recapitulated.def: past tense of recapitulate; summarized again
recapitulated.sentence: He recapitulated the main points of the meeting in an email.

recapitulation.def: a summary or concise review of the main points
recapitulation.sentence: The final chapter provides a recapitulation of the entire book.

recede.def: to move back or away from a previous position
recede.sentence: The floodwaters began to recede after the storm passed.

recidivism.def: the tendency of a convicted criminal to reoffend
recidivism.sentence: Programs aimed at reducing recidivism have shown promising results.

recidivist.def: a person who relapses into criminal behavior
recidivist.sentence: The judge sentenced the recidivist to a longer prison term.

reciprocal.def: given, felt, or done in return
reciprocal.sentence: They have a reciprocal agreement to share resources.

reckless.def: without thinking or caring about the consequences of an action
reckless.sentence: His reckless driving caused a serious accident.

recluse.def: a person who lives a solitary life and tends to avoid other people
recluse.sentence: The famous author became a recluse after her book was published.

reclusive.def: avoiding the company of other people; solitary
reclusive.sentence: He led a reclusive life in a remote cabin in the woods.

recoil.def: to suddenly move back because of fear or disgust
recoil.sentence: She recoiled in horror at the sight of the spider.

recompense.def: to pay or reward someone for effort, loss, or harm
recompense.sentence: The company offered to recompense her for the damage to her car.

reconcile.def: to restore friendly relations or make compatible
reconcile.sentence: After their argument, they tried to reconcile their differences.

reconciliation.def: the restoration of friendly relations or harmony
reconciliation.sentence: The treaty brought about a lasting reconciliation between the two nations.

recondite.sentence: The professor's lecture on quantum mechanics was so recondite that few students could follow it.

reconnaissance.def: military observation of a region to locate an enemy or ascertain strategic features
reconnaissance.sentence: The pilot flew a reconnaissance mission over enemy territory.

reconnoiter.def: to make a preliminary observation or survey of
reconnoiter.sentence: The scouts were sent to reconnoiter the area before the main force arrived.

recourse.def: a source of help or security in a difficult situation
recourse.sentence: When negotiations failed, they had no recourse but to go to court.

recrimination.def: an accusation in response to an accusation from someone else
recrimination.sentence: The debate ended in a bitter exchange of recriminations.

rectify.sentence: The error was quickly rectified by the technician.

rectitude.def: morally correct behavior or thinking; righteousness
rectitude.sentence: He was a man of unquestioned moral rectitude.

recumbent.def: lying down in a position of comfort or rest
recumbent.sentence: She spent the afternoon in a recumbent position on the sofa.

red-letter day.def: a special, happy, or important day
red-letter day.sentence: Graduation day was a red-letter day for the entire family.

redeem.def: to compensate for the faults or bad aspects of something
redeem.sentence: The ending of the movie redeemed an otherwise boring plot.

redolent.def: strongly reminiscent or suggestive of something
redolent.sentence: The old house was redolent of a bygone era.

redouble.sentence: The team redoubled their efforts in the final minutes of the game.

redoubtable.sentence: The redoubtable champion defeated all challengers.

redound.def: to contribute greatly to a person's credit or honor
redound.sentence: His heroic actions redound to his lasting fame.

redress.def: remedy or compensation for a wrong or grievance
redress.sentence: The victims sought redress through the legal system.

reductive.def: tending to present a subject in a simplified form, especially one viewed as crude
reductive.sentence: The reductive analysis failed to capture the complexity of the issue.

redundant.def: not or no longer needed or useful; superfluous
redundant.sentence: The old equipment became redundant after the upgrade.

refine.def: to remove impurities or unwanted elements from
refine.sentence: The company refines crude oil into gasoline.

refracted.def: bent or changed direction when passing from one medium to another
refracted.sentence: The refracted light created a rainbow on the wall.

refractory.sentence: The refractory child refused to obey any commands.

refurbish.sentence: They decided to refurbish the old house instead of tearing it down.

refute.def: to prove a statement or theory to be wrong or false
refute.sentence: The scientist refuted the claim with solid evidence.

regale.def: to entertain or delight someone with stories or performances.
regale.sentence: The old sailor regaled us with tales of his adventures at sea.

regimen.def: a prescribed course of medical treatment, diet, or exercise for health or fitness.
regimen.sentence: Her daily regimen includes a morning run and a strict diet.

regress.def: to return to a previous, less advanced state.
regress.sentence: After months of progress, the patient began to regress under stress.

reinstate.def: to restore someone or something to a previous position or condition.
reinstate.sentence: The company decided to reinstate the employee after the misunderstanding was cleared.

reiterate.def: to say or do something again or repeatedly.
reiterate.sentence: The teacher reiterated the instructions to ensure everyone understood.

rejoicing.def: great joy or happiness, often expressed in a lively way.
rejoicing.sentence: There was widespread rejoicing when the peace treaty was signed.

rejoinder.def: a quick, witty, or sharp reply.

rejuvenated.def: made to feel or look younger, fresher, or more lively.
rejuvenated.sentence: After a week at the spa, she felt completely rejuvenated.

relegate.def: to assign to an inferior rank or position.
relegate.sentence: The team was relegated to the lower division after a poor season.

relent.def: to become less severe, harsh, or strict; to yield.
relent.sentence: The storm finally relented after three days of heavy rain.

relentless.sentence: The relentless pursuit of excellence drove her to succeed.

relevant.def: closely connected or appropriate to the matter at hand.
relevant.sentence: Please provide only relevant information for the report.

relinquish.def: to voluntarily give up or let go of something.
relinquish.sentence: He had to relinquish control of the company after the merger.

relish.def: to enjoy greatly; to take pleasure in.
relish.sentence: She relished the opportunity to travel abroad.

remedial.def: intended to correct or improve a skill or condition.
remedial.sentence: The student took remedial classes to improve his math skills.

reminiscent.def: suggesting or reminding one of something.
reminiscent.sentence: The scent of the flowers was reminiscent of her grandmother's garden.

remiss.def: negligent or careless in performing a duty.
remiss.sentence: It was remiss of me not to call and confirm the appointment.

remonstrate.sentence: The citizens remonstrated against the new tax law.

remorse.def: deep regret or guilt for a wrong committed.
remorse.sentence: He felt remorse for his harsh words.

remote.def: far away in distance or time; isolated.
remote.sentence: The cabin was located in a remote area of the forest.

remunerated.def: paid or compensated for work or services.
remunerated.sentence: She was well remunerated for her consulting work.

remuneration.def: payment or compensation for services rendered.
remuneration.sentence: The remuneration for the job was generous.

renaissance.def: a revival or rebirth, especially of culture or learning.
renaissance.sentence: The city experienced a cultural renaissance in the 1990s.

rend.def: to tear or split apart violently.
rend.sentence: The explosion rent the building in two.

render.def: to provide or give a service, help, or a verdict.
render.sentence: The jury rendered a verdict of not guilty.

renege.def: to go back on a promise or commitment
renege.sentence: He reneged on his promise to help with the project.

renounce.def: to formally give up or reject something
renounce.sentence: She renounced her claim to the throne.

renowned.sentence: The renowned scientist received a Nobel Prize.

rent.def: a regular payment for the use of property
rent.sentence: They paid the rent on the first of every month.

renumeration.def: payment or compensation for services rendered
renumeration.sentence: The consultant received generous remuneration for her work.

reparation.def: the act of making amends for a wrong or injury
reparation.sentence: The company offered reparations to the victims of the spill.

repast.def: a meal, especially a large or formal one
repast.sentence: They enjoyed a lavish repast at the banquet.

repent.def: to feel regret or remorse for one's actions

repercussion.def: an unintended consequence of an event or action
repercussion.sentence: The layoffs had serious repercussions for the local economy.

repertoire.def: the range of skills or pieces that a person is prepared to perform
repertoire.sentence: The pianist's repertoire included works by Chopin and Liszt.

replenish.sentence: We need to replenish our food supplies before the storm.

replete.sentence: The book is replete with historical inaccuracies.

repose.def: a state of rest or tranquility
repose.sentence: She found repose in the quiet garden.

repress.sentence: He tried to repress his anger.

reprimand.sentence: The employee received a reprimand for being late.

reprisal.sentence: The attack was in reprisal for the earlier bombing.

reprise.def: a repetition of a performance or action
reprise.sentence: The band played a reprise of their hit song.

reproach.def: an expression of blame or disappointment
reproach.sentence: She looked at him with reproach.

reprobate.sentence: The old reprobate spent his days gambling.

reprove.sentence: The teacher reproved the student for talking in class.

repudiate.sentence: He repudiated the accusations against him.

repugnant.sentence: The idea of eating insects is repugnant to many people.

repulse.sentence: The army repulsed the enemy attack.

reputed.sentence: He is reputed to be the best surgeon in the city.

requisite.def: required or necessary for a particular purpose.
requisite.sentence: A college degree is a requisite for many professional jobs.

requite.def: to repay or return something, especially a feeling or action.
requite.sentence: She requited his love with unwavering devotion.

rescind.def: to revoke, cancel, or repeal a law, order, or agreement.
rescind.sentence: The company rescinded its job offer after discovering the applicant's misrepresentation.

reservation.def: a doubt or qualm about something; also, an arrangement to have something held for one's use.
reservation.sentence: He had reservations about the plan but agreed to proceed.

resignation.def: the act of giving up a job or position; also, passive acceptance of something undesirable.
resignation.sentence: She submitted her resignation after the conflict with management.

resigned.def: having accepted something unpleasant that cannot be changed.
resigned.sentence: He was resigned to the fact that he would not get the promotion.

resilient.def: able to recover quickly from difficult conditions.
resilient.sentence: The resilient community rebuilt after the devastating earthquake.

resolute.sentence: She remained resolute in her decision to run for office despite the criticism.

resolution.def: a firm decision to do or not do something; also, the quality of being determined.
resolution.sentence: His New Year's resolution was to exercise daily.

resolve.def: to settle or find a solution to a problem; also, to decide firmly on a course of action.
resolve.sentence: They worked together to resolve the dispute.

resolved.sentence: She was resolved to finish the marathon despite her injury.

resonant.def: deep, clear, and continuing to sound; also, having a strong emotional effect.
resonant.sentence: His resonant voice filled the auditorium.

resourceful.def: able to deal effectively with different situations, especially difficult ones.
resourceful.sentence: The resourceful traveler found a way to fix the broken engine with limited tools.

respectively.sentence: John and Mary scored 85 and 90, respectively.

respite.def: a short period of rest or relief from something difficult or unpleasant.
respite.sentence: The cool breeze provided a welcome respite from the heat.

resplendent.def: attractive and impressive through being richly colorful or sumptuous.
resplendent.sentence: The queen was resplendent in her gold and diamond jewelry.

restitution.def: the restoration of something lost or stolen to its proper owner; also, recompense for injury or loss.
restitution.sentence: The court ordered the thief to make restitution to his victims.

restive.def: restless and uneasy; also, impatient under restriction.
restive.sentence: The audience grew restive during the long speech.

restrictive.def: imposing restrictions or limitations on someone's activities or freedom.
restrictive.sentence: The restrictive dress code prohibited jeans and sneakers.

resurgence.def: an increase or revival after a period of little activity or popularity.
resurgence.sentence: There has been a resurgence of interest in vinyl records.
